Title: The Key Man: The True Story of How the Global Elite Was Duped by a Capitalist Fairy Tale
Devil's Coin: My Battle to Take Down the Notorious OneCoin Cryptoqueen
Where the Axe Is Buried
Review: Where The Axe Is Buried by Ray Nayler--An aggressively poignant look at the nature of totalitarian regimes, this story explores a near future world where custom AI Prime Ministers make decisions for the country. There's a lot of ideas presented here, the book is both timely and timeless, examining tactics of totalitarianism which (the author alleges) increasingly apply to the West. I thought the perspectives brought forward here were very well-written, with soaring prose as well as a tacit acceptance, almost boredom, with egregious impositions on liberty.  Nayler invites the reader to consider the ways in which preconceived notions impact perceptions of how the world should be, both for individuals and for nation-states. I look forward to reading this again. Two thumbs up.

Title: Great Minds Don't Think Alike: Debates on Consciousness, Reality, Intelligence, Faith, Time, Ai, Immortality, and the Human
The Perfect Divorce
Review: The Perfect Divorce by Jeneva Rose--An excellent follow-up to an excellent book. The story here focuses on a woman years after she failed to represent her husband in the murder trial where he was accused of murdering his mistress. She has now married another man, and a new murder threatens to change everything. Shifting perspectives between the protagonist, her husband, and the police investigation is a highlight, and I can't wait to see what's to come. Thumbs up.

Title: All the Birds in the Sky
Off the Edge: Flat Earthers, Conspiracy Culture, and Why People Will Believe Anything
Best Offer Wins
Review: This book is a domestic thriller, centered on questioning how far people will go for the American dream. The story focuses on an upper middle class couple looking to start a family in an increasingly out of reach housing market, a familiar tale to many. The obsession that follows, however, hopefully remains far from even the most devoted reader. Thumbs up.

Title: Shutter Island
Review: Shutter Island by Dennis Lehane--I haven't seen the movie, so I don't know how it compares, but dang this is fun. It's dark and twisted (in multiple senses of the word) and the thought that went into the story is incredible. Extremely impressive...and I may need a movie night sometime soon. Thumbs up.

Title: Murder the Truth: Threats, Intimidation, and a Secret Campaign to Protect the Powerful
Review: Murder the Truth by David Enrich--This is a very different Enrich book. Enrich has previously followed financial trails and open records in pursuit of information on scammers, lawyers, banks, and more. This book, instead tracks the ongoing efforts to attack the first amendment. There's a whole lot of legal issues at stake here, and Enrich does yeoman's work in making this analysis accessible to the layperson while still getting at the core principles. I appreciated in particular his connecting the dots across multiple cases; he points out the ouroboros of cases where a Supreme Court justice references a lower court in his dissent while that lower court judge references the SC justice in future cases, creating a miasma of confusion around precedent and settled law for future courts to consider. It's an urgent book, released at an urgent moment. Thumbs up.

Title: Star Wars Inquisitor: Rise of the Red Blade
We Are Electric: Inside the 200-Year Hunt for Our Body's Bioelectric Code, and What the Future Holds
Unmasking the Administrative State: The Crisis of American Politics in the Twenty-First Century
Polybius
Review: Polybius by Collin Armstrong--This book feels vaguely like an SCP post fleshed out. It's an intense, frightening story centered around the latest arcade game in town that people can't get enough of. As time goes on, obsession intensifies and the game increases its hold on the community. I didn't love how the ending resolved, but I think that's primarily a question of preference. I'm mostly interested in if other books of this type exist. Thumbs up.

Title: The Known Citizen: A History of Privacy in Modern America
How to Sell a Haunted House
Review: How To Sell A Haunted House by Grady Hendrix--Rippling with action, this is a book about real estate travails. It's also a meditation on grief and an examination of stories--both the stories we tell ourselves and the deceptions we embrace in order to exist as a community, in this case, a family. The book is paced well, and written delightfully with horror punching through the pages to tap you on the shoulder. I think both narratively and emotionally, it's quite well done. Two thumbs up.

Title: Burn-In: A Novel of the Real Robotic Revolution
The Games Gods Play
Review: The Games Gods Play by Abigail Owens--The concept for this book is Hunger Games meets Greek mythology. Done well, that concept alone feels like it prints money. Done poorly, it looks instead like this. Characters feel like walking talking tropes while the underlying romance plot is both pervasive and distracting. The whole story is poorly set up, and there's a love triangle that can't exist from an "edit" perspective because the protagonist's options are someone who is dead, someone with severe allergies (??), and the god of death himself, who apparently is the winner. I started this on a walk this morning and almost gave myself the Christmas gift of not finishing it. Be kinder to yourself than I was. Thumbs down.

Title: The Information: A History, a Theory, a Flood
The Eye of the Bedlam Bride
The Secret History of Wonder Woman
The Years of Rice and Salt
Review: Alternate history is dominated by western-centric views, and this book is a refreshing alternative from a master of writing. I love Robinson's other work (most notably Ministry for the Future, which is one of my favorite depictions of the near future that I've read), but this is something astounding. It imagines a world where the "Black Death" wipes out nearly all instead of "merely" a third of folks alive in Europe at the time. The story then continues over a whirlwind of people and places over centuries of development. It's a touching work in many respects. Thumbs up.

Title: Duped: Why Innocent People Confess and Why We Believe Them
When the Moon Turns to Blood: Lori Vallow, Chad Daybell, and a Story of Murder, Wild Faith, and End Times
Review: When the Moon Turns To Blood by Leah Sottile--I have been thinking about this review for a few days now, it's actually stopped me from reading anything else. In general, it's a good book about an absolutely horrific series of crimes committed by members of an extremist LDS cult. But the questions it raises are profound and even unsettling. The core thing I took away was wondering how "true believers" in extreme eschatological positions can ever truly be in community with the rest of society. For example, in this case, if you truly believe the Apocalypse or Rapture or Tribulation is imminent, and you have any particular personal or religious goals, it becomes irrational (to the irrational mind) to let society, laws, or any other institution stand between you and those goals. This book demonstrates the furthest extent of that sort of thought, but a little really goes a long way. I need to think a little more about my takeaways here. Regardless, it's a good book, though sometimes reads like the script of a cheesy true crime podcast, which is a bit off-putting. I think the author also writes trying to shed light on questions she chooses to ask that have no answer. Trying to find reason in this is like asking why a hurricane happens, you can point to the atmospheric pressure changing and all these logical factors, but someone asking that question is just trying to understand the end result, the devastation. There's no answer for that, at least not in this life. Perhaps after the Apocalypse occurs, we'll have better answers. Thumbs up.

Title: Girl One
Review: This speculative fiction book imagines a world where parthenogenesis was successful in humans in the 1970s. More than that, though, it's a parable about belonging, marginalization, and power. It's a compelling book that's paced very well, akin to a thriller.

Title: A Game for All the Family
Newsroom Confidential: Lessons (and Worries) from an Ink-Stained Life
Breaking and Entering: The Extraordinary Story of a Hacker Called Alien
Heretical Fishing
Review: Heretical Fishing by Haylock Jobson--This book earnestly replicates my personal experience playing Stardew Valley. A regular dude shows up in a new world that has a cozy town featuring people with a variety of issues, and his response is a strong desire to fish. When I began playing Stardew Valley, I was playing with a friend, and I very selfishly dabbled in farming and otherwise just went to the dock every single day and fished all day. I'd cast the rod, reel in a fish, repeat. Hours and hours. Mindless rod movement that got me excited every time. This book captures that experience that I previously thought was mine and mine alone perfectly. I couldn't be happier. Thumbs up.

Title: These Silent Woods
Review: These Silent Woods by Kimi Cunningham Grant--Mysterious and foreboding, this book explores the highs and lows of living alone in the woods with your child. Turns out there's a lot of lows. I never wholly got into this book, and I think a large reason for that is a large misunderstanding which clearly creates the central conflict. Once you strip that away, there's no tension, the characters aren't all that compelling, and it becomes a morality tale with a subpar ending. Thumbs down.

Title: Money: The True Story of a Made-Up Thing
The Second Estate: How the Tax Code Made an American Aristocracy
Patriarchy Blues: Reflections on Manhood
Review: Patriarchy Blues by Frederick Joseph--Moving and passionate, this book collects essays and poems largely on the patriarchy. Some of these could be full chapters or books in their own rights. Others are powerful because of their brevity. Joseph here is very vulnerable and clear in his critiques of our system. Thumbs up.

Title: We Burn Daylight
Review: We Burn Daylight by Bret Anthony Johnston--This fictional account of 1993 Waco is riveting. In part, the appeal comes from knowing how the story ends. But it's also a darn good story in its own right. Lively, even challenging characters get caught up in the goings-on of Waco while a modern day thread recreates the missing pieces. In many ways, this is about family, principles, and how a legacy is left. Thumbs up.

Title: Thank You for Being Late: An Optimist's Guide to Thriving in the Age of Accelerations
Democracy's Data: The Hidden Stories in the U.S. Census and How to Read Them
Burn Book: A Tech Love Story
Review: Burn Book by Kara Swisher--Irreverent yet poignant, this book explores Swisher's rise to preeminence in tech journalism. Swisher does not allow even the veneer of false modesty to pass her lips, instead framing herself as sage and oracle of all future tech trends, conveniently. In some respects, this book is the journalism version of that distant relative who always regales the family with takes of the investments he narrowly missed out on. There's some salacious anecdotes which justify the book, but I wouldn't recommend it. Sideways thumb.

Title: The Employees: A Workplace Novel of the 22nd Century
Devolution: A Firsthand Account of the Rainier Sasquatch Massacre
Review: Devolution by Max Brooks--This is really fun, and as opposed to World War Z, which was almost an oral history retelling a zombie apocalypse, this is the book version of a found footage movie. The structure is weird, but it works. It focuses on a connected but isolated utopia that begins to fall apart in the wake of volcanic activity. The pacing is fun, the story is great, very enjoyable. Thumbs up.

Title: The Inevitable: Understanding the 12 Technological Forces That Will Shape Our Future
Voodoo Histories: The Role of the Conspiracy Theory in Shaping Modern History
Review: Voodoo Histories by David Aaronovitch--Insightful and entertaining, this book explores the world of conspiracy theories. It primarily does this by dissecting particular theories throughout the last century, noting who spread them, who benefited from it being spread, and offering common sense, noncomprehensive takedowns of the theories. At the same time, Aaronovitch draws a throughline demonstrating where these theories start, why they spread, and the impact they can tend to have on the historical record. He also makes some implicit connections between critiques of history generally (that is, that dominant "winners" and rule-setters tend to write the histories and therefore the history itself should be looked at critically) and a modern prevalence of conspiracy theories of all kinds. It was a thought-provoking book. Thumbs up.

Title: The Magic Christian
Review: The Magic Christian by Terry Southern--Short and sweet, this satire of American commercialism skewers power, money, and desire through quick vignettes, all through the perspective of a capricious billionaire. Finding parallels to today is a pretty simple task, and the book's quick pacing and weird tone makes it all the more thought-provoking. Thumbs up.

Title: The Good Life: Lessons from the World's Longest Study on Happiness
The Plot
Review: The Plot by Jean Hanff-Korelitz--This begins as a book about the writing process and evolves into a carefully crafted thriller. The book is fun and inviting throughout while the twist is foreshadowed enough that I'm already looking forward to the sequel in a few weeks. Thumbs up.

Title: Propaganda, Inc.: Selling America's Culture to the World
The Age of Stagnation: Why Perpetual Growth Is Unattainable and the Global Economy Is in Peril
Everything I Need to Know I Learned in the Twilight Zone: A Fifth-Dimension Guide to Life
Radicalized: Four Tales of Our Present Moment
Crash Landing: The Inside Story of How the World's Biggest Companies Survived an Economy on the Brink
The Milky Way Smells of Rum and Raspberries
There Is No Antimemetics Division
Review: There Is No Antimemetics Division by qntm--I've described other books as feeling evocative of the SCP Foundation project, but this book truly feels ripped from the wiki. It's a collection of somewhat cohesive short stories that explore (sometimes literally) unfathomable threats in the world. There's a lot of heart here, but I think some of it gets obscured by the jargon-heavy world building. For that reason, it's hard for me to recommend unless you already have a keen interest in the paranormal. Sideways thumb.

Title: The Age of Surveillance Capitalism: The Fight for a Human Future at the New Frontier of Power
When Did You See Her Last?
For Profit: A History of Corporations
What Is Real?: The Unfinished Quest for the Meaning of Quantum Physics
Mr. President, How Long Must We Wait?: Alice Paul, Woodrow Wilson, and the Fight for the Right to Vote
For Blood and Money: Billionaires, Biotech, and the Quest for a Blockbuster Drug
Maid: Hard Work, Low Pay, and a Mother's Will to Survive
Review: Maid by Stephanie Land--Touching and scathing, Land here relates a deeply personal story of an unplanned pregnancy which thrust her into poverty. From navigating enigmatic government aid programs to dealing with capricious, even abusive, employers, Land's story is hard to read, but is an eye-opening, urgent discussion of poverty in America and the hope she found in her own path. Thumbs up.

Title: Rationality: What It Is, Why It Seems Scarce, Why It Matters
Raising Them Right: The Untold Story of America's Ultraconservative Youth Movement--And Its Plot for Power
With All Due Respect: Defending America With Grit and Grace
The Mercy of Gods
Review: The Mercy of Gods by James S. A. Corey--Sometimes I make mistakes when choosing which books to read. This book was certainly a mistake. I'm riveted. And I simply do not have the patience to wait for the next books to release. I really appreciated the author's prior Expanse series and the change of tone and pace for this book just deepens that appreciation. A sprawling epic is in store, across many worlds, with a fun cast of characters, and this book, unfortunately for me, is just the beginning. Thumbs up.

Title: Intelligent Love: The Story of Clara Park, Her Autistic Daughter, and the Myth of the Refrigerator Mother
Pretty Girls
Review: Pretty Girls by Karin Slaughter--This book made me a believer in nominative determinism. It's also incredibly dark and gory. Maybe more than any other book I've read? Graphic descriptions of attacks on women permeate the book, and the subject matter is, at best, grim. Good story though. Thumbs up.

Title: No Longer Human
Review: No Longer Human by Osamu Dazai--It's unsurprising that this is a classic in Japan. This is framed as a series of journals documenting the life of a man living a rather ordinary life. But in its matter-of-fact banality, there's a lot of power. The protagonist feels lost in a world that's both changing and drawing folks toward conformity. The feeling of isolation or alienation is universal, a first principle that this gets at well. Thumbs up.

Title: Six Angry Girls
Review: Six Angry Girls by Adrienne Kisner--This is a fun story about high school girls navigating romance, extracurriculars, and knitting. It features young women in drama and mock trial, two causes very near to my heart, but something didn't click for me. I think it's primarily that the different characters who have voices in the story didn't sound all that different, and it made the narrative somewhat muddled. I wouldn't recommend this book, but it wasn't bad! Sideways thumb.

Title: Nickel and Dimed: On (Not) Getting by in America
Review: Nickel and Dimed by Barbara Ehrenreich--This book is a classic in observing how the lower class survives, or doesn't. Ehrenreich was a journalist who conducted an experiment where she worked in a variety of low-wage jobs, struggling to find affordable housing and pay for basic essentials. She writes in a largely self-deprecating way about herself, but with tremendous care about the workers she toils alongside. The book is striking in its relevance. After all, the inflation-adjusted wages she was earning throughout 2000 across the country are still significantly higher than what many similarly-situated folks are earning now. In some cases, her exact same income levels could be easily earned today, the only change is that housing and basic essentials are more expensive than ever. It's a solid, short work. Thumbs up.

Title: Benjamin Franklin's Last Bet: The Favorite Founder's Divisive Death, Enduring Afterlife, and Blueprint for American Prosperity
Apocalypse: How Catastrophe Transformed Our World and Can Forge New Futures
No Human Contact: Solitary Confinement, Maximum Security, and Two Inmates Who Changed the System
The Five Year Lie
Review: The Five Year Lie by Sarina Bowen--Action-packed and full of heart, this book is about a single mother who is an heiress to a large doorbell camera company. From ethics in surveillance and law enforcement to being able to opt out of a society that opts many in to a lot of things by default, the story covers a lot of ground. It is thrilling and delightful to the very end. Thumbs up.

Title: Tomorrowmind: Thriving at Work with Resilience, Creativity, and Connection—Now and in an Uncertain Future
Authors: Gabriella Rosen Kellerman, Gabriella Rosen Kellerman, Martin Seligman, Martin Seligman

Title: The Internet Con: How to Seize the Means of Computation
Review: The Internet Con by Cory Doctorow--While I generally agree with the arguments Doctorow makes here about interoperability and copyright/DMCA overreach, he makes sweeping dismissals of legitimate counterarguments in largely unappealing ways. I get that this is a short book by a small publisher, but spending just a page or two on each of the counterarguments you're choosing to present doesn't let you tackle the best version of those arguments, it's an easy recipe for strawmanning, which is present here in spades. Sideways thumb.

Title: Manual for Survival: A Chernobyl Guide to the Future
Review: Manual for Survival, A Chernobyl Guide To The Future by Kate Brown--Not particularly interested in relitigating the cause of the Chernobyl disaster, Brown instead explores the aftermath. From coverups and whitewashing to devastation and exposure, she explores the medical/scientific community's response to the crisis. There was a lot here I didn't know, including the weather manipulation missions conducted by Russia which moved radioactive rain away from major Russian cities into Belarus. The book is insightful, and drawn from a great deal of firsthand experience and interviews. Thumbs up.

Title: Star Wars: The Rise and Fall of the Galactic Empire
Let the Lord Sort Them: The Rise and Fall of the Death Penalty
Book of Night
Review: Book of Night by Holly Black--The concept is fun, this is about a woman who can manipulate shadows, but in an otherwise gritty/real world. Something didn't quite hit for me, and I honestly don't know what. I think it's just that the magical/urban fantasy isn't as much my thing. I thought from the description that I might be able to overcome it, unfortunately I was wrong. Sideways thumb.

Title: The House of Silk
Review: The House of Silk by Anthony Horowitz--As the book jacket proudly proclaims, this is the first book authorized by the Arthur Conan Doyle estate in its 125 year history. And what a book it is. Horowitz captures Doyle's tone and style remarkably well while simultaneously piecing this story into the broader Doyle canon. It's exhaustively researched, paced well, and any Doyle fan will find something to love in this book. Thumbs up.

Title: Exposure: Poisoned Water, Corporate Greed, and One Lawyer's Twenty-Year Battle Against DuPont
The Woman Who Fooled The World: Belle Gibson's Cancer Con, and the Darkness at the Heart of the Wellness Industry
Take Your Breath Away
Review: Take Your Breath Away by Linwood Barclay--I decided to give the author another shot after he came highly recommended by a friend. This book is way more of what I was looking for. After a man's presumptively dead wife reappears six years after her disappearance, he finds himself questioning a lot of things. This time, the reveals feel more earned (even if still a bit contrived), and the pacing was significantly better. Thumbs up.

Title: The Monsters Know What They're Doing: Combat Tactics for Dungeon Masters
Review: The Monsters Know What They're Doing by Keith Ammann--Encyclopedic, this book explores ever basic type of monster in Dungeons And Dragons' Fifth Edition. Along the way, Ammann details the strategy tips and thought process that each type of creature might have. He helpfully focuses on what he sees as the organic combat strategies of most creatures, for example, a creature with low intelligence might behave more instinctually, a creature with resistance to certain damage types might organically fear wielders of that damage less, and so forth. This is a useful resource for any dungeon master, and my only regret is getting it from the library rather than purchasing a copy for myself. I'll go rectify that now. Thumbs up.

Title: How to Hide an Empire
Review: How To Hide An Empire by Daniel Immerwahr--Fast-paced, with lightning wit, Immerwahr dissects the history of the American empire. He explores the acquisition and maintenance of overseas American interests, and how and why our collective approach changed over time. There's some astonishing stuff here. I didn't realize that the Constitution we drafted for Japan post WWII is still in existence today (with no amendments!) and that, accordingly, the Japanese people are actually guaranteed more rights, including gender equality, than we are. Immerwahr also gets into Pearl Harbor specifically--many US interests were attacked simultaneously, yet only Hawaii was focused on. He examines draft FDR speeches for the "day of infamy" that included the Philippines, for example, but FDR opted to exclusively look at Pearl Harbor. It's really interesting and thought-provoking. Thumbs up.

Title: We Are Bellingcat: Global Crime, Online Sleuths, and the Bold Future of News
Counting: How We Use Numbers to Decide What Matters
Unlocked: An Oral History of Haden's Syndrome
Review: Unlocked by John Scalzi--A short and gripping oral history, this work follows the initial detection and progression of the disease eventually known as Haden's Syndrome (discussed in further detail in Scalzi's Lock In). It's a worthwhile follow-up/prequel, humanizing multiple figures referred to in Lock-in. Thumbs up.

Title: In Deep: The FBI, the CIA, and the Truth about America's "Deep State"
Boys Enter the House: The Victims of John Wayne Gacy and the Lives They Left Behind
Strangers in the car
Review: This tense, propulsive thriller was really well-paced, with twists and turns that keep the reader going. I thoroughly enjoyed this.

Title: Superman: President Luthor
Contributors: Doug Mahnke, Mark Schultz, Dale Eaglesham, Peter David, Matthew Clark, J.M. DeMatteis, Arthur Adams, Ed McGuinness, Tony Harris, Greg Rucka

Title: How to Live Safely in a Science Fictional Universe
Lost At Sea: The Jon Ronson Mysteries
Antisocial: Online Extremists, Techno-Utopians, and the Hijacking of the American Conversation
The Dark Corners of the Night
Review: A challenging look at a fictional young serial killer with psychopathy.

Title: Beyond Biden: Rebuilding the America We Love
Ghosts of Iron Mountain: The Hoax That Duped America and Its Sinister Legacy
Never Let Me Go
Review: Never Let Me Go by Kazuo Ishiguro--This is apparently one of the top ten books of the 21st century according to the New York Times. While it's certainly a moving book, I think the hype around it (and associated lengthy hold times with my local library) is impossible to live up to. It's a very good piece of speculative fiction from beginning to end, but in my limited experience, I'm not sure what differentiates this from other great works of speculative fiction, other than possibly the heart behind it. Because that care, and joy, shines through at every turn in what could be a very dark story. Regardless, it's a fun and worthwhile book. Thumbs up.

Title: Life 3.0: Being Human in the Age of Artificial Intelligence
When We Cease to Understand the World
Review: When We Cease To Understand The World by Benjamin Labatut--Call it fictional nonfiction, or a history that doesn't care all that much about being right. This is an acclaimed examination of mathematicians and scientists who fundamentally shaped our lives today, from Schrodinger's famous paradox to Heisenberg's uncertainty principle. The author is less interested in these breakthroughs than the impact they had on the individuals. In short, what does it mean for you, as an individual, to be the only person in the world, however briefly, who truly understands the world (in your view). After all, many people feel this way. Cult leaders, mentally unstable folks, the narcissist in your workplace, all of these feel they may have a unique claim to the truth. This is a thought-provoking and, in some ways, deeply sorrowful work. Thumbs up.

Title: The Explanation for Everything
Review: The Explanation for Everything by Lauren Grodstein--Touching and reflective, this book explores the life of an evolutionary biologist and his deeply religious student. Grodstein writes with significant empathy for relevant characters, those with deeply held religious convictions, those challenged with the emptiness of the universe, those struggling with alcoholism. It's a story about flawed characters, written with a lot of heart. Thumbs up.

Title: Repeat After Me
Review: Repeat After Me by Jessica Warman--This book about a high school field trip gone woefully wrong is very fun. It's a time loop book (as an aside, I feel like time loops are the new quicksand in that they're something we get a lot of cautionary tales about while being somewhat unlikely to encounter them in the world) with fun characters. Some people really do just exist to be caricatures rather than fully fleshed out characters, but tonally, that works with this book. Thumbs up.

Title: Monopoly X: How Top-Secret World War II Operations Used the Game of Monopoly to Help Allied POWs Escape, Conceal Spies, and Send Secret Codes
The Daily Grind
Review: I'm uncertain if I want to continue with this. The world building is interesting and there are some threads it would be interesting to see in future books. But the interpersonal conversations between characters are insufferable, especially when the author gets into their inner thoughts. Really a dilemma.

Title: Starry Messenger: Cosmic Perspectives on Civilization
Spell It Out: The Curious, Enthralling and Extraordinary Story of English Spelling
Digital Cash: The Unknown History of the Anarchists, Utopians, and Technologists Who Created Cryptocurrency
The Rise and Fall of the Dinosaurs: A New History of a Lost World
Empire of Pain: The Secret History of the Sackler Dynasty
What If Jesus Was Serious?: A Visual Guide to the Teachings of Jesus We Love to Ignore
The Psychopath Test: A Journey Through the Madness Industry
Devil House
Review: Devil House by John Darnielle--Grounded fictional true crime is the best descriptor I've got for this book. The story follows a successful true crime writer who is on the hunt for his next story. In some parts, the book is stream of consciousness, in others it's a hard-hitting depiction (and critique) of true crime journalism. In those respects, it's a tough read. But the book is also so interested in creating the appearance of a "real book" that I think Darnielle loses the forest in favor of some distinct trees. Frustratingly, though there's a lot of potential here, I didn't enjoy the experience, and am glad to have something else to do on this beautiful Saturday. Thumbs down.

Title: The New India: The Unmaking of the World's Largest Democracy
Review: The New India by Rahul Bhatia--This book gives a clear, frightening look at India as it exists today. Bhatia was interested in documenting why India rose and then began to fall as a democracy. In doing so, he constructs a character-driven portrait of autocracy and nationalism on the rise with Indian institutions subsumed in the pursuit of a single unifying identity for the country. It's a very good narrative look at modern day India. Thumbs up.

Title: The Physics of Superheroes
Review: The Physics of Superheroes by James Kakalios--Honestly I was surprised by how much I enjoyed this book. More than merely a scientific explainer of various physics principles relevant to the Marvel and DC universes, this is a love letter to the Silver Age of superheroes. From the obscure to the renowned, heroes and villains of all stripes get their (scientific) justice in this joy-filled, fact-laden explainer. Thumbs up.

Title: Trillion Dollar Triage: How Jay Powell and the Fed Battled a President and a Pandemic---andPrevented Economic Disaster
Free to Focus: A Total Productivity System to Achieve More by Doing Less
This America: The Case for the Nation
The Fear Index
Review: The Fear Index by Robert Harris--Much like some Crichton books hold up less the more their underlying concepts become or stray away from reality, this book began ahead of its time and now may be behind it. As a thriller, it's very fun, with an unreliable protagonist running a high-value investment firm. It's relatively short and I didn't regret reading it. I also wouldn't want to penalize a decent thriller that can't stand the test of time. Thumbs up.

Title: Nexus: A Brief History of Information Networks from the Stone Age to AI
Review: Nexus by Yuval Noah Harari--Neither the triumphant anthropological hit of Sapiens nor the stuttering repetition of Harari's follow-up, Homo Deus, this book is something entirely different. Harari presents a framework of information, namely that information of all kinds can be used to gain truth or order, and that order and truth can be used to gain wisdom and power. These ideas are somewhat esoteric and rely in part on unspoken "higher order" ideals. This is true particularly when one reads Harari's discussion of the coming "canonization" of AI, that is, that what we accept as fundamentally true about AI has not been written yet and that there is still time to come to some sort of societal or communal framework for it. While this is true, I'm not certain it necessitates a book. So I'm left with an interesting but largely unsatisfying construction, in short, I'm not sure Harari's versions of truth or order grant either power or wisdom to the reader. Sideways thumb.

Title: The Familiar
Review: The Familiar by Leigh Bardugo--I am somewhat uncertain why I came back to the well. I know this book came recommended by some, but it really is not up my alley. However, I tried this, didn't love it. More than just the genre itself, Bardugo juggles a growing cast of characters poorly. The protagonist is fleshed out, but everyone else has an agenda or ambition that only exists through her own existence. The world, then, is narrow and cramped, without much room to explore. Sideways thumb.

Title: The Bond King: How One Man Made a Market, Built an Empire, and Lost it All
SPQR: A History of Ancient Rome
Review: SPQR by Mary Beard--Exploring the life and times of the Roman empire, this book is a sweeping portrait of a millennium of history. Crucial to the success of this book is the author explaining how we know what is known about the Roman empire, and what gaps there are in that knowledge. One stunning achievement here is that Beard refrains from speculating about the fall of Rome, a topic that traps most who write about it. Instead, the economics, hobbies, and social circles of Roman life are all fair game, and we are better for it. Thumbs up.

Title: The In-Between: Unforgettable Encounters During Life's Final Moments
Your Face Belongs to Us: A Secretive Startup's Quest to End Privacy As We Know It
Six Wakes
Review: Six Wakes by Mur Lafferty--This really is an elevation of the locked room mystery.  First, most importantly, I had a great bowl of tortellini while reading this book, and that memory will stick with me for awhile. I also got eliminated from an online reality game while reading it, and so the high-stakes nature of a battle for survival in space was really salient. This book was extremely reminiscent of the excellent books by Stuart Turton, including The Last Murder at the End of the World. The pace is great, featuring people who, for various reasons, don't know why death has occurred, and the information is teased out at just the right moments. I thoroughly enjoyed the experience of reading this. Thumbs up.

Title: The Quiet Damage: QAnon and the Destruction of the American Family
Review: The Quiet Damage by Jesselyn Cook--I raced through this book, stopping at some discrete moments to tear up, sitting in a house I'm not wholly familiar with in McKinney, Texas. Cook follows men and women around the country caught in QAnon. Mercifully, Cook doesn't waste a lot of time explaining what QAnon is, instead providing masterfully concise primers in the stories themselves. This allows her to spend time taking in the toll that the movement has taken on human lives across the country. Spouses torn apart, friendships demolished, parents estranged from their children, and so on. This quiet, but real, damage is tough to engage with, but this is a wholly worthwhile book. Two thumbs up.

Title: Make Russia Great Again
Review: Make Russia Great Again by Christopher Buckley--How do you parody a president who defies satire? Poorly, as it turns out. This book, written from the perspective of a sycophantic chief of staff to former President Trump, really makes the reader feel for late night hosts that had to come up with fresh material on the Trump administration night after night. Released in the midst of COVID-19, this book exists in that almost unbelievably space of being too over the top and also not accurate enough. Parodies of people, places, and events are astonishingly on point for a piece of satire, yet the whole endeavor is listless. Thumbs down.

Title: Accidental Saints: Finding God in All the Wrong People
Smoke Kings
Review: Smoke Kings by Jahmal Mayfield--This is a rough book in many respects. The characters often exhibit open animus, largely on racial lines. It focuses on a group of four conspirators who set out to implement their own version of reparations through violence and coercion. Transparently, I didn't like any of the characters here, but the story itself is compelling. Thumbs up.

Title: The Plot Against America
Review: The Plot Against America by Philip Roth--This fictional alternate history of the United States is difficult to put down. It changes one primary thing in American history--in 1940, as America's involvement in WWII seems more certain, Charles Lindbergh runs for President against FDR and wins, driving and enabling overt, targeted antisemitism. The story is told from the perspective of a Jewish family in New Jersey and is quite well researched. Truthfully, the book is more prescient than it even appears on the surface. Given the people who are referenced and the situations folks find themselves in, I expected this to be a book researched and completed in the last few years, not 20 years ago in 2004. There's a lot to consider here, but I think Roth does a good job of demonstrating how out-group marginalization can happen quickly and without warning, an idea which rings true at all times, but especially six months before an election. Thumbs up.

Title: Several People Are Typing
Review: Several People Are Typing by Calvin Kasulke--Work sucks. That's what this book tries to capture. The whole text of the book is a series of Slack conversations, and the most uncomfortable parts of interacting with colleagues digitally and in person are captured aptly here. I think the book handles human connection really well, and it was a great start to the morning. Thumbs up.

Title: As Gods: A Moral History of the Genetic Age
Above Top Secret: Uncover the Mysteries of the Digital Age
Serpent on the Rock
Review: Serpent on the Rock by Kurt Eichenwald--Legitimately breathtaking in parts, this book chronicles the meteoric rise of profits for Prudential, as well as its downfall as the investments it was hawking were systematically revealed to be scams. Tens of thousands of people lost nearly all their retirement savings due to a combination of a relentless pursuit of profits and a seeming epidemic of willful blindness. It's an infuriating book, written around the time I was born. The company has recovered from this scandal greatly. Prudential is currently the largest insurance provider in the United States. They were able to use profits from other areas of the business to occasionally compensate individuals for the massive fraud they were sold. None of the executives directly involved ended up seeing jail time. If no good deed goes unpunished, perhaps it's also true that no bad deed goes punished. Nevertheless, thumbs up.

Title: Easy Money: Cryptocurrency, Casino Capitalism, and the Golden Age of Fraud
I Need You to Read This
Review: I Need You To Read This by Jessa Maxwell--This take on a psychological thriller is really fun. When a woman unexpectedly finds employment replacing a recently murdered advice columnist, spooky things happen. There's a lot of themes running through this, but I really liked the throughline of the psychology of advice columns.  Why do we reach out for help, what brings us to that point? This is a fun, easy read to round out the year. Thumbs up.

Title: Glass Houses
Review: Glass Houses by Madeline Ashby--This is a very fun thriller, with twists and turns throughout. It focuses on a tech startup celebrating the sale of their new emotion-tracking algorithm when tragedy strikes. It's very Black Mirror meets Ruth Ware, with a little bit of Margaret Atwood thrown in to boot. It's a rollicking ride that I can't say much more about for fear of spoiling things. Thumbs up.

Title: A Brief History of Vice: How Bad Behavior Built Civilization
The Great Stewardess Rebellion: How Women Launched a Workplace Revolution at 30,000 Feet
The Essential Scalia: On the Constitution, the Courts, and the Rule of Law
Prequel: An American Fight Against Fascism
Chokepoints: American Power in the Age of Economic Warfare
The Practice, the Horizon, and the Chain
Review: The Practice, The Horizon, and The Chain by Sofia Samatar--Lyrical and smooth, this book can be read in one sitting but packs a punch. In some ways, it's unmoored from time and place, in others, it's inextricably chained to our ideas about ourselves and the future. I thought this was delightful. Thumbs up.

Title: The Office of Mercy
Contributors: Emily Woo Zeller (Narrator), Emily Woo Zeller (Narrator)

Title: Let the People Pick the President: The Case for Abolishing the Electoral College
The Fifth Domain: Defending Our Country, Our Companies, and Ourselves in the Age of Cyber Threats
Authors: Richard A. Clarke, Robert K. Knake

Title: Counting the Cost
Authors: Derick Dillard, Jill Duggar, Craig Borlase

Title: The Other Valley
Review: The Other Valley by Scott Alexander Howard--This book begins in what seems like a thought experiment gone wrong. A lovely hamlet borders, to its east and west, hamlets which are exactly the same, but exist twenty years in the future and past, respectively. The book feels derivative in some ways of other books like Sea of Tranquility. But I do feel as though it stands on its own merits. Thumbs up.

Title: The Madness
Review: The Madness by Dawn Kurtagich--It's been a bit since I've been delighted by a story in quite this way. This book, about a psychiatrist battling her own demons when she gets pulled into an investigation of a mysterious illness, surprises in the best ways while keeping a sense of foreboding throughout. I feel bad that I had to delay finishing this for so long, but this book was worth the wait. Zero spoilers here, but come in with an open heart and mind, and settle into spooky season. Two thumbs up.

Title: The Unraveling
Review: The Unraveling by Vi Keeland--This is a book about a woman that does NOT follow the APA's ethical principles for psychologists, and in many ways is a cautionary tale about straying away from strict codes of conduct. It's an incisive psychological thriller that simultaneously plumbs the depths of grief and guilt. I really enjoyed this book, it's a quick read that packs a punch. Thumbs up.

Title: Information Wars: How We Lost the Global Battle Against Disinformation and What We Can Do about It
The LEGO Story: How a Little Toy Sparked the World's Imagination
Edge of Reality: Journeys Through the Rabbit Hole of Reality Television
When Everyone Knows That Everyone Knows . . .: Common Knowledge and the Mysteries of Money, Power, and Everyday Life
Suspect: An Olympic Bombing, the Fbi, the Media, and Richard Jewell, the Man Caught in the Middle
Date-Onomics: How Dating Became a Lopsided Numbers Game
The Life Impossible
Review: The Life Impossible by Matt Haig--Soaring. Triumphant. Absolutely charming. Two thumbs up.

Title: Where the Deer and the Antelope Play: The Pastoral Observations of One Ignorant American Who Loves to Walk Outside
Review: Where the Deer and the Antelope Play by Nick Offerman--I came expecting a typical celebrity memoir and instead encountered a rousing discussion of Aldo Leopold's land ethic. Leopold has been one of my niche interest areas for awhile, and seeing him covered in this form is refreshing. Offerman is down to earth, engaging in a full-throated defense of conservation. Thumbs up.

Title: The Chain
Review: The Chain by Adrian McKinty--Devastating and fast-paced, this book is about a woman on the brink of losing everything. Her cancer has returned, and her daughter has been kidnapped by a mysterious group that asks her to pay a ransom and kidnap another child, continuing The Chain. These are rich characters, despite the extremely action-packed premise. The ending isn't wholly satisfying, but the ride there is a delight. Thumbs up.

Title: Find You First
Review: Find You First by Linwood Barclay--This book is fun but somewhat disjointed. It tries to manufacture a mystery from a tech CEO, a pedophile, and some mercenaries, but fails to build enough connective tissue in the process. Storylines running in parallel that comes together at the end don't work well here, and as a general matter, I never got invested enough in the narrative. Sideways thumb.

Title: All in Her Head: The Truth and Lies Early Medicine Taught Us about Women's Bodies and Why It Matters Today
Mỹ Documents
Review: My Documents by Kevin Nguyen--Hauntingly banal, this book presents a fictional account of a modern day internment camp. It's light on details about the inciting incident, focusing instead on the experiences of Vietnamese Americans surviving the camps and those who remain relatively unscathed. I think Nguyen is able to bring out the impact societal trauma can have on a marginalized population, whether particular individuals experience that trauma or not. I'm going to be thinking about this book for a while. Thumbs up.

Title: Second Glance
Review: Second Glance by Jodi Picoult--Told initially through a series of related vignettes in a small Vermont town, this book is a psychological thriller which, among other things, explores the horrors of eugenics in early 20th century America. While the story itself is well-told and powerful, it's undercut a bit by the ending. There's a natural end point the book gets to...and the story just keeps going. With that said, I'd still recommend it. Thumbs up.

Title: The Myth of Capitalism: Monopolies and the Death of Competition
Like, Literally, Dude: Arguing for the Good in Bad English
You're It: Crisis, Change, and How to Lead When It Matters Most
Authors: Joseph M. Henderson, Eric J. McNulty, Leonard J. Marcus

Title: You Bet Your Life: From Blood Transfusions to Mass Vaccination, the Long and Risky History of Medical Innovation
American Psychosis: A Historical Investigation of How the Republican Party Went Crazy
The Mother Next Door: Medicine, Deception, and Munchausen by Proxy
Curtains on A Christmas Carol: A Cozy Mystery
Flat Earth
Review: Flat Earth by Anika Jade Levy--A quick and mighty whirlwind, this book avoids subtlety like the plague. Set in a hyper present world that feels like it's closing in, the story touches on the commodification of all while cheapening everything. The protagonist seems to lose her sense of self, of worth, to the pressures of the world around her. It's an exercise in running in place with impending doom taking over everyone and everything around you. In that sense, it's a perfect story of the day. Thumbs up.

Title: Proof: The Uncertain Science of Certainty
Holly
Review: Holly by Stephen King--I've read some Stephen King books, but not a lot. That makes this book, which focuses on a character who apparently plays a role in several other King books, an odd choice for me, but I saw it at Books-A-Million. I really enjoyed this book. A thriller with the answer given to the reader from the start, instead of worrying about whodunnit, you get to plumb the depths of these very fun and full characters. The COVID throughline of this book places it at a very specific time and place, and I'm not certain that always services the story. It constantly gives the characters something to react to, giving them, uh, character, I guess, but more often than not, it's just distracting. With that said, the story is a lot of fun. Thumbs up.

Title: UNSUB
Review: This book is paced very well, and I loved its approach to getting in the mind of a killer. It's a Criminal Minds episode on steroids.

Title: Some Remarks: Essays and Other Writing
The Sanatorium
Review: The Sanatorium by Sarah Pearse--We are so back. This book is about a luxury hotel opened at the location of a closed sanatorium. However, Pearse adeptly cuts from the past to the present, and the story is told efficiently but in a thrilling way. No complaints, it was a good book. Thumbs up.

Title: An Economist Walks Into a Brothel: And Other Unexpected Places to Understand Risk
Dark Money: The Hidden History of the Billionaires Behind the Rise of the Radical Right
In Plain Sight: The Startling Truth Behind the Elizabeth Smart Investigation
The Need
Review: The Need by Helen Philipps--You think you know where this book is going and then it takes a sharp turn. This was a really fun book that kept me guessing, and I appreciated that the author was willing to let readers sit with unanswered questions. This is largely a domestic thriller that's most interested in the minutiae of day-to-day life. Thumbs up.

Title: The Heat Will Kill You First: Life and Death on a Scorched Planet
Review: The Heat Will Kill You First by Jeff Goodell--Unflinchingly resonant, this book examines the climate crisis from the human perspective in a really skillful way. Goodell's experience in this area shines through as he interviews top environmental scientists, and his journalistic skill shows as he communicates the experiences of people--right here, right now--suffering because of the climate crisis, warning of the horrors he sees coming. It's a disheartening look at our present and a terrifying look at our future. Two thumbs up.

Title: Arriving Today: From Factory to Front Door -- Why Everything Has Changed About How and What We Buy
Review: Arriving Today by Christopher Mims--Globe-spanning yet focused, this book tracks the efforts in technological innovation, automation, and human labor cost that it takes to move one USB charger from production in Vietnam to a gone address in the United States. Mims, going from ship to port to long haul trucker to warehouse, finds incredible technology which has allowed ever-increasing consumer demands to grease the spinning wheels of capitalism. At the same time, he finds human lives, often under compensated and overworked, bending and even breaking under the stresses of these demanding roles. It's a fascinating, necessary look at delivery logistics in the modern world. Thumbs up.

Title: Murder in the Family
Review: Murder in the Family by Cara Hunter--This book features a lot of diagrams, newspaper clippings, etc. as it invites you to solve the mystery alongside fictional forensic experts on a fictional true crime program. The conceit is a lot of fun, and while I wouldn't recommend reading this book digitally with some of the small text in the diagrams, the book really throws you into the attempt to solve a cold case. I thought this was really well done overall. Thumbs up.

Title: The Puzzle Box
Review: The Puzzle Box by Danielle Trussoni--There are books about difficult puzzles that are done well. This isn't one of them. Characterization is poor, perhaps even simplistic, of a guy who's good at puzzles and needs to go to Japan to solve one. It's difficult to see any particular character (including the daughters of Satoshi Nakamoto) as doing anything other than existing as a puzzle piece. Plug and play. Poor characterization, a really forced storyline, and it's capped off by the fact that reading about someone moving tiles around on a puzzle box is never going to be that interesting. Thumbs down.

Title: Secluded Cabin Sleeps Six
Review: Secluded Cabin Sleeps Six by Lisa Unger--A weekend getaway gone wrong. The trope itself is perhaps overdone, but it's executed well here, in a thriller about family and mixing business and pleasure. The characters were fully realized, with intentions and secrets that were consistent. However, at its core, it's a psychological thriller. If that's not your vibe, this book won't change your mind. If it is, add this to your TBR. Thumbs up.

Title: Wired for War: The Robotics Revolution and Conflict in the Twenty-First Century
Digging Up Armageddon: The Search for the Lost City of Solomon
The Long Alliance: The Imperfect Union of Joe Biden and Barack Obama
I See You've Called in Dead
Creators Take Control: How Nfts Revolutionize Art, Business, and Entertainment
When the Clock Broke: Con Men, Conspiracists, and How America Cracked Up in the Early 1990s
Review: When the Clock Broke by John Ganz--Context is key, right? In this entertaining, thought-provoking book about the 90s, Ganz certainly seems to think so. He tracks how conspiracists, con men, and intellectuals ended up transforming the mindset of the country, delivering a concurrent beat-by-beat look at culture and contemporary historians which informed the era. This is well done in many respects, not least of which because this is a book about Trump and the forces that pushed him to power while rarely mentioning his name. Along the way, David Duke, Pat Buchanan, Ross Perot, and America's shifting relationship with the world take center stage with some conclusions that I think will stay with me long after this book returns to the digital library. Two thumbs up.

Title: The Inner Clock: Living in Sync with Our Circadian Rhythms
Review: The Inner Clock by Lynne Peeples--Both a history of our relationship with sleep and an analysis of the science behind it, this book is fascinating. Everything from window shape to breastfeeding to time of medicine ingestion is fair game in this wide-sprawling review, and the stories inside are riveting. I enjoyed some of the meta-analysis of studies on sleep and performance across a variety of metrics. Thumbs up.

Title: Zero Days
Review: Zero Days by Ruth Ware--This book feels desperately frantic. Ware does a great job of starting off with raised stakes that continue until the very end. As a reader, you almost breathe a sigh of relief when the book concludes. A psychological thriller, the story focuses on a security/penetration tester who works in tandem with her husband until circumstances have her gallivanting around the UK to solve a murder. This was maybe Ware's most predictable book I've read, the story and endgame was pretty clear about a third of the way in. But even knowing what's ahead, the ride is superb. It's a tech-forward book that's still immensely accessible. Thumbs up.

Title: Quantum Night
Review: Quantum Night by Robert J. Sawyer--I mean, this is quantum mechanics meets an in-depth exploration of ethics, all framed in some ludicrous plot devices that somehow still hit way too close to home. Of course I loved this. The book follows an applied philosophy professor who gets the chance to, erm, apply some philosophy when he discovers a nearly two decade old memory gap. The plotting for this story is bananas, but it's a fun read. Thumbs up.

Title: Hype: How Scammers, Grifters, and Con Artists Are Taking Over the Internet--And Why We're Following
Gregor and the Code of Claw
What Would Gen-Z Do?: Everything You Don't Know about Gen-Z But Should
Wild Dark Shore
Review: Wild Dark Shore by Charlotte McConaghy--Vivid, with language that leaps off the page and into your soul, this book is both thriller and scientific exploration. The story is simple, a woman arrives on an isolated island which will soon be consumed by rising water. The story itself is well-constructed, undergirded by words that are in turns soaring and despairing. I'll be thinking about this for awhile. Two thumbs up.

Title: Bad Blood: Secrets and Lies in a Silicon Valley Startup
The Last Days of Jack Sparks
Who Knows You by Heart
Review: Who Knows You By Heart by C. J. Farley--The second-person perspective in this book is certainly jarring, but rewarding. Farley's thumb is on the pulse of Big Tech here while simultaneously being full of nearly absurd hope. You'll love the singular point of view of the protagonist and the surprisingly earnest responses to near-atrocities from the largest companies (oligarchy?) you already depend on. You take this book in as a story of stories encapsulating a lot of big ideas, including a serious contemplation of where you go from here. Thumbs up.

Title: River of the Gods: Genius, Courage, and Betrayal in the Search for the Source of the Nile
Being Online: On Computing, Data, the Internet, and the Cloud
Broken Signposts: How Christianity Makes Sense of the World
Polostan
Review: Polostan by Neal Stephenson--This is a very solid start to what promises to be an epic trilogy. It's historical fiction, set primarily in the 1920s and 30s in the United States. I really enjoyed the story and the characters (particularly the protagonist), who still remain somewhat of an enigma. There's a lot of depth to the narrative, and I'm excited to see what comes next. Thumbs up.

Title: All the Secrets of the World
Future Home of the Living God
The Biggest Ideas in the Universe 1: Space, Time and Motion
Anatomy of a Con Artist: The 14 Red Flags to Spot Scammers, Grifters, and Thieves
Dead in Long Beach, California
Review: Dead In Long Beach, California by Venita Blackburn--I was not sold on the idea of this book initially. As I got further in, however, the story was at turns poignant, then hilarious, then deep. With one foot in the past and one foot in the future, Blackburn turns a real wallowing in grief into something greater, and the end result is a book that's fundamentally weird but delightful. Thumbs up.

Title: Daedalus Is Dead
Review: Via a series of quick vignettes, this book explores the life (and death) of Daedalus from beginning to end. It's touching in its approach and succeeds in drawing the reader in.

Title: Lying in State: Why Presidents Lie -- And Why Trump Is Worse
The Fury
Review: The Fury by Alex Michaelides--I thought Michaelides' The Maidens was a little lacking after loving his Silent Patient, this book puts him right back into the mix. The beginning of the plot comes straight from a Knives Out movie: an eccentric star takes her friends to her private Greek island. From there, however, the story turns to a character-focused drama, led first and foremost by the narrator, a burgeoning playwright. The story skillfully weaves from point to point like a sailor navigating the fierce winds around the Greek isles, leaving no stone unturned in a relentless march toward a conclusion which, in retrospect, seems obvious. Two thumbs up.

Title: The Library: A Catalogue of Wonders
The Woman in Cabin 10
Review: The Woman in Cabin 10 by Ruth Ware--I can't stop raving about Ware. Every book I've read by her has been phenomenal, and this was no exception. This was a more straightforward murder mystery than I've previously seen of hers, and it's impeccably crafted. The story is riveting, and it's backed up by an ongoing ticking clock. Even though Ware doesn't include her typical atmospheric dread, the pacing is relentless and the party don't stop until the very last page. Two thumbs up.

Title: The Checklist Manifesto: How to Get Things Right
Review: The Checklist Manifesto by Atul Gawande--Truly I cannot think of two works by the same author that hit such different notes tonally. Being Mortal by Gawande was an emotionally resonant book about aging and the ways we as a society and medical profession fail the oldest among us. This book, by contrast, is a manual for the private sector masquerading as a more general self-help book. That's not to say that it's not worthwhile, the anecdotes, largely from the medical and airline industries, demonstrate the author's idea that well-designed checklists save lives. Maybe I need to be less haphazard when packing for work trips. Thumbs up.

Title: The Rational Optimist: How Prosperity Evolves
My Murder
Review: Thrilling yet contemplative, this book imagines a world where cloning exists for people who have died whom society has an interest in bringing back. The protagonist, a woman recently murdered by a prolific serial killer, is one of these clones, and must solve her own murder. This book is really only one small part murder mystery, however, and gives a much more in-depth focus to the things that drive us. When we've literally lost everything and gained it back, how do we cope with that? It's a solid, inventive book. Thumbs up.

Title: The Art of Language Invention: From Horse-Lords to Dark Elves, the Words Behind World-Building
Review: The Art of Language Invention by David Peterson--Language invention is wild. This book dives extremely deep into constructed languages. I've never thought so much about glottal sounds and the alveolar sounds that make up the ways we communicate. The material is thorough, and if you want a primer on everything to consider in making your own language, this is the book for you. Thumbs up.

Title: Still Christian: Following Jesus Out of American Evangelicalism
After Evangelicalism: The Path to a New Christianity
All Jobs and Classes! I Just Wanted One Skill, Not Them All! (LITRPG/EPIC PROGRESSION):
Goliath’s Curse: The History and Future of Societal Collapse
Boldly Go: Reflections on a Life of Awe and Wonder
Thinking Inside the Box: Adventures with Crosswords and the Puzzling People Who Can't Live Without Them
Sherlock Holmes & The Three Winter Terrors
How the World Made the West: A 4,000-Year History
Review: How the World Made the West by Josephine Quinn--A surprisingly comprehensive examination of world history, this book has a lofty goal: demonstrate that the origins of "the West" are far more murky than then Minoans, Myceneans, and Greco-Roman ties we've been taught. Quinn does this through a fascinating overview of Indian literature, Babylonian law codes, and Phoenician artifacts, alongside accounts from the Greeks and Romans themselves acknowledging their culture was (of course) one step of many in the human experience. It's a delightful book. Thumbs up.

Title: Preparing for War: The Extremist History of White Christian Nationalism--and What Comes Next
Review: Preparing for War by Bradley Onishi--This book cuts deep. It's an examination of White Christian nationalism found throughout American evangelicalism both from a historical lens and from the personal experiences of the author. Onishi details a layered, broad-spanning movement rooted in a simultaneous terrified fear of losing worldly power with an unshakeable sense of entitlement to that power among the evangelical Right/White. Onishi powerfully ties the bow on the story with the culmination of January 6 and his own analysis of what's to come. This book is worth the read. Thumbs up.

Title: The Wager: A Tale of Shipwreck, Mutiny, and Murder
Your Consent Is Not Required: The Rise in Psychiatric Detentions, Forced Treatment, and Abusive Guardianships
Vicious
Review: Vicious by V.E. Schwab--Oddly sentimental in parts, this book takes place in a very bloody universe where superheroes operate mostly in the shadows, wreaking havoc on the world. The crux of the story focuses on college roommates who discover their new powers and take wildly divergent paths. This is a dark story with a lot of power. Thumbs up.

Title: When the Moon Hits Your Eye
The Rise and Reign of the Mammals: A New History, from the Shadow of the Dinosaurs to Us
Theodore Roosevelt for the Defense: The Courtroom Battle to Save His Legacy
Right Kind of Wrong: The Science of Failing Well
Review: Right Kind of Wrong by Amy Edmondson--It's tough for me to imagine the person or group this wouldn't be helpful for. With a solid blend of scientific data and demonstrative anecdotes, this book addresses failure from multiple points of view--group/system, individual, and societal. It truly is chock-full of good information, much of which gets glazed over on the rush to the next insight. This is a good place to land for a thoughtful primer on what failure is and how best to think about it. Thumbs up.

Title: Kill Switch: The Rise of the Modern Senate and the Crippling of American Democracy
Where They Wait
Review: Where They Wait by Scott Carson--Mindbending and strange, this book follows a meditation app gone wrong. When a journalist learns about a novel calmness app that's making waves, he soon gets sucked in. This is dark and twisty, I really had fun reading it. Thumbs up.

Title: The Cult of We: Wework, Adam Neumann, and the Great Startup Delusion
Review: The Cult of We by Eliot Brown--Y'know, as someone who works for an agency that's been targeted by Wall Street Journal reports lately, there's some schadenfreude present in reading this charting of the rise and fall of WeWork. This book repackages and expands on WSJ reporting with some really jaw-dropping stuff. Ultimately, this was a story of hubris and self-dealing, with a surprising amount of messianic tendencies. Thumbs up.

Title: Everyday Utopia
Review: Everyday Utopia by Kristen Ghodsee--This is a fun, thought-provoking book about our ever-so-human attempts to create utopia and our ever-so-human failures at every turn. Ghodsee reviews two thousand years of communes, collaborative schools, rural and urban experiments, all trying to get at a "better" way of living. While the book is well-researched, much time is spent amplifying the successes of these experiments when it's possible a more edifying book would detail why these attempts didn't work out. Ghodsee is sometimes militantly evangelical in her approach, and she appears convinced that the glorification of the family unit is the primary thing that keeps us one step away from utopia. To that end, she brings up philosophers, revolutionaries, and, yes, cult leaders who have also decried the family unit. I think there's certainly something to what she's saying--what does it mean for the American political system to be focused on winning "middle class/working class families" (even if only in word, not in deed) rather than uplifting everyone through a communal approach? For me, though, I think this book convinced me that a society-level communal utopia isn't possible to strive for. Put another way, maybe the real utopia is the friends we made along the way. Thumbs up.

Title: The Loop: How Technology Is Creating a World Without Choices and How to Fight Back
Merchants of Doubt: How a Handful of Scientists Obscured the Truth on Issues from Tobacco Smoke to Global Warming
The Examiner
Review: The Examiner by Janice Hallett--I saw this book on a trip to the bookstore to see my good friend who is an author and a bookstore employee. His books are great, and so is this book. The story plunges you into the position of an independent grade reviewer who is poring through messages and assignments submitted for an art class. There's an alleged death and a twist that I was delighted by, and the message fragments, class schedules, and notes make the book immersive and very fun. Thumbs up.

Title: Revenge of the Tipping Point: Overstories, Superspreaders, and the Rise of Social Engineering
Review: Revenge of the Tipping Point by Malcolm Gladwell--I came in optimistic that this would be a full-throated defense of tipping culture, and was excited to learn more. Instead I got something best described as some interesting ideas thrown in a microwave and lazily stirred together, served up without a thesis and a garnish. The book lives or dies on telling a cohesive story, it's a book about the idea of overarching metanarratives, after all. And in this case, it dies. Fine anecdotes, bad book. Sideways thumb.

Title: The Gospel of J. Edgar Hoover: How the FBI Aided and Abetted the Rise of White Christian Nationalism
Five Days at Memorial: Life and Death in a Storm-Ravaged Hospital
The God Who Is There
Authors: Francis A. Schaeffer, James W. Sire

Title: Empire of Imagination: Gary Gygax and the Birth of Dungeons & Dragons
FORWARD: Notes on the Future of Our Democracy
How Do I Tax Thee?: A Field Guide to the Great American Rip-Off
Unmask Alice: LSD, Satanic Panic, and the Imposter Behind the World's Most Notorious Diaries
Review: Unmask Alice by Rick Emerson--Enthralling and emotional, Emerson explores the Satanic panic through the lens of a woman who duped the world into believing what they already wanted to believe. Beatrice Sparks claimed to have edited the diary of a girl struggling with drug addiction, turning the adaptation into a book called Go Ask Alice, but as Emerson demonstrates, that book and much of her subsequent success was fabricated from whole cloth. It's a wild book, dealing with drug abuse, the Satanic panic, suicide, and law enforcement. Thumbs up.

Title: Somewhere Beyond the Sea
Review: Somewhere Beyond the Sea by TJ Klune--I'm holding back tears as I'm writing this. I don't think I've ever been more moved by a book. Analytically, this sequel to The House in the Cerulean Sea works because the reader already knows the characters well and little preamble is necessary. But more to the point, the story, the characters, the setting, it all combines to be...triumphant. Joyfully so. Unapologetically so. It stirs righteous anger while dripping unlike anything I've ever read with pure, unadulterated charm. I've read a lot of "feel-good" books, I've never read anything quite like this. Two thumbs up.

Title: American Idolatry: How Christian Nationalism Betrays the Gospel and Threatens the Church
Lost in Math: How Beauty Leads Physics Astray
Review: Lost In Math by Sabine Hossenfelder--Challenging but accessible, this book examines the beauty fallacy, the idea, according to the author, that scientists get caught up in the beauty of equations, believing that an equation must be beautiful/simple in order to describe the universe at large. I got somewhat lost in the discussion of quantum mechanics, but in general, her point seems to be that some assumptions about how the universe might work aren't driven from any laws we know, but rather an implicit bias toward beauty and simplicity that dominates science as a whole. It's a fascinating idea, and one that is discussed thoroughly in these pages. Thumbs up.

Title: Coldwire
Review: I didn't love the perspective shifting here. It's an interesting first book to a trilogy, but I don't think I'll continue.

Title: The Amazing Story of Quantum Mechanics: A Math-Free Exploration of the Science that Made Our World
Review: The Amazing Story of Quantum Mechanics by James Kakalios--Charming and informative, this book explores quantum mechanics through the framing of comics written contemporaneously with some of the biggest scientific discoveries of the twentieth century. This framing device works well, as Kakalios can dive into what's wrong--and surprisingly, what's right--about the pseudoscience that underpins the origins of heroes and villains both renowned and obscure. The explanations are delightful and down-to-earth, and Kakalios goes out of his way to connect dots and show how our understanding of quantum mechanics has led to everyday improvements in our lives. Thumbs up.

Title: Gods and Mortals: Ancient Greek Myths for Modern Readers
Long Island Compromise
Review: Long Island Compromise by Taffy Brodessor-Akner--Steeped in Jewish-Americsn family life, this book tackles a whole lot of subjects and handles them with charm without shying away from their heaviness. Trauma, the meaning of a life well-lived, capitalism, and a family's collective experience are all served up and lived in this book that almost feels like the pages melt away while reading. I don't normally love this kind of book, but I do recommend it. Thumbs up.

Title: How the Bible Actually Works: In Which I Explain How An Ancient, Ambiguous, and Diverse Book Leads Us to Wisdom Rather Than Answers—and Why That's Great News
A Well-Trained Wife: My Escape from Christian Patriarchy
Review: A Well-Trained Wife by Tia Levings--This is a tough read. Cult-like groups, sexual abuse, it's all fair game in this memoir by a woman who loses herself in her religious tradition. Viewers of Shiny Happy People will appreciate the details, reviewing the teachings of Bill Gothard, who leads the Duggar family and others. It ripples with themes like control and resilience, while being, for lack of a better phrase, really sad. Thumbs up.

Title: The God of the Woods
Review: The God of the Woods by Liz Moore--With a scope that's simultaneously minute and sweeping, this book tells the story of a girl who vanishes from her summer camp in upstate New York. The drama unfolds over decades through shifting perspectives, remaining satisfying the whole time. This was a really good first thriller of the year. Thumbs up.

Title: Affirming: A Memoir of Faith, Sexuality, and Staying in the Church
An Immense World: How Animal Senses Reveal the Hidden Realms Around Us
Alpha Girls: The Women Upstarts Who Took on Silicon Valley's Male Culture and Made the Deals of a Lifetime
All the Light We Cannot See
Book Madness: A Story of Book Collectors in America
Madness: Race and Insanity in a Jim Crow Asylum
Review: Madness by Antonia Hylton--Truly heartwrenching, this book is about atrocities and lessons from a segregated asylum in Maryland. The asylum began on the backs of its patients--the first folks to be committed to the institution were tasked with building it. From there, the book documents injustices on racial lines through desegregation. This is one of the most sobering books I've read this year, and the author does a good job of telling the story of where we are societally today. Thumbs up.

Title: When McKinsey Comes to Town: The Hidden Influence of the World's Most Powerful Consulting Firm
Come & Get It
Review: Come and Get It by Kiley Reid--This book, by the author of Such a Fun Age, is a fun, though dark, college/coming of age story. It explores young men and women who are away from home for the first times, and Reid skillfully depicts those experiences. However, not much happens during the book itself, and weirdly, for a book so heavily focused on a wide array of characters, no one seems to learn anything from their shared or private experiences. Perhaps Reid is trying to say something about the fairly static nature of the human condition. But if I wanted to see relatively uninteresting things happen to relatively uninteresting people while in the end, nothing really ends up happening, I'd attend more work meetings in Washington DC. Sideways thumb.

Title: Chasing Evil: Shocking Crimes, Supernatural Forces, and an FBI Agent's Search for Hope and Justice
The MAGA Diaries: My Surreal Adventures Inside the Right-Wing (And How I Got Out)
Everyone in My Family Has Killed Someone
No One Is Talking About This
Review: No One Is Talking About This by Patricia Lockwood--Segmented yet free-flowing, this book reads as a poem moves and a river persists. One observation then on to the next with no time to digest anything you're taking in. This could be distracting in other books but here is largely the point, as Lockwood is discussing the Internet broadly and how our communal addiction is shaping culture. She's also talking about heartbreak, and loss, and pain, in the context of a world that often feels like it's moving for movement's sake. The references she makes are wry and insightful, and the story is perhaps uniquely powerful. Thumbs up.

Title: All That Remains
Review: All That Remains by Sue Black--Embued with a deep sense of calm, this book explores the act of dying. Black is a forensic anthropologist who, in the process of telling her life's story, ends up examining human nature, rituals around death, and the act of leaving a legacy. It's a sober-minded evaluation of death while being a poignant mediation on life. Thumbs up.

Title: Batman: Three Jokers
Contributors: Rob Leigh (Contributor), Jason Fabok (Illustrator), Brad Anderson (Illustrator)

Title: To Raise a Boy: Classrooms, Locker Rooms, Bedrooms, and the Hidden Struggles of American Boyhood
Review: To Raise A Boy by Emma Brown--This is a book about myths and how we respond to them, at least in part. Brown writes here about boys and their underreported experiences of sexual violence, the wide disparity in suicidality between men and women, and the sometimes faltering steps of the educational system to address these issues. I was struck most of all by some of the unconscious biases Brown talks about, where the ways we think about men and women have disparate impacts. She also shares the evidence on interventions at a community or school level that are proven to have results. It's a thought-provoking read. Thumbs up.

Title: You Are a Badass: How to Stop Doubting Your Greatness and Start Living an Awesome Life
Tender Is the Flesh
Review: Tender is the Flesh by Agustina Bazterrica--Chilling as can be, this book imagines a world where all animal meat is contaminated by a disease lethal to humans, resulting in humans being bred to serve as the only available source of meat. It's a predictably grim world populated by terrible people. The protagonists exist in the world but feel separate from it, with lives marked by tragedy. Everything leads to an ending I felt like I should have seen coming, but didn't. The vibes are really what this book is about, and the vibes are not good. The book, however, is. Thumbs up.

Title: Going Infinite: The Rise and Fall of a New Tycoon
To Hold Up the Sky
Review: Often wide in scope and singularly focused on impact, this collection of short stories will be familiar to anyone reading Liu again. Broad and galaxy-spanning problems have specific consequences for individuals the reader can quickly invest in. Each story, despite these constraints, remains relatively unique, and even beautiful. Thumbs up.

Title: Sex, God, and the Conservative Church: Erasing Shame from Sexual Intimacy
The Man Who Loved Books Too Much: The True Story of a Thief, a Detective, and a World of Literary Obsession

Review: 2054 by Elliot Ackerman and Admiral James Stavridis--I expected from these authors a grounded look at future warfare. Instead, I got Ray Kurzweil as a character in an absolutely insane political thriller with naive idealism rivaling Aaron Sorkin. Was this a fun read? Yes, actually. Was it a worthwhile one? To bastardize Kurzweil himself, "Death gives meaning to our lives. A lackluster book, then, is worse than death." Sideways thumb.

Title: Bye, Baby
Review: Bye, Baby by Carola Lovering--Chillingly enthralling, this book explores a friendship gone awry and families torn asunder. Very few books I've read are as unputdownable (which I'm still convinced is a made up word) as this one. It's a perilously slow burn, with enough reveals along the way that the story continues to draw you in. I'm displeased modestly with some of the resolution (or lack thereof) but it doesn't take away at all from the fun psychological thriller. Thumbs up.

Title: Why Is This Night Different from All Other Nights?
This Is How They Tell Me the World Ends: The Cyber Weapons Arms Race
Desiring the Kingdom: Worship, Worldview, and Cultural Formation
Duped: Double Lives, False Identities, and the Con Man I Almost Married
Genius Makers: The Mavericks Who Brought A.I. to Google, Facebook, and the World
The Trap
Review: The Trap by Catherine Ryan Howard--Relatively unique among thrillers I've read lately, this book is dripping with tension. The stories of a woman whose sister is missing, a civilian employee of the Irish police department, and the killer himself narrating his own journey all coalesce in this story that has a twist fitting delightfully in place. For those looking for satisfying, well-resolved endings, you might be disappointed, but I really enjoyed this book. Thumbs up.

Title: The End of the World Is Just the Beginning: Mapping the Collapse of Globalization
Nobody's Child: A Tragedy, a Trial, and a History of the Insanity Defense
Dark Wire: The Incredible True Story of the Largest Sting Operation Ever
