Review: Dark Wire by Joseph Cox--Diving inside one of the largest sting operations in history, this book details the cat and mouse game played by the FBI and a consortium of other international intelligence agencies with criminal rings around the world. It's a fascinating story, focusing on the FBI's development of its own encrypted communication app which it maneuvered groups into using. Interesting and thrilling. Thumbs up.

Title: Kochland: The Secret History of Koch Industries and Corporate Power in America
Sleep Tight
Review: Sleep Tight by J. H. Markert--This thriller is a lot of fun. It really epitomizes an intense "race against time" story, and while there's elements that I didn't love, it's still a fun time. Some overly contrived plot points tarnish the story, pieces that fit a little too neatly together. But it's spooky and enjoyable. Thumbs up.

Title: Conversations with Rbg: Ruth Bader Ginsburg on Life, Love, Liberty, and Law
The Book of Love
Review: The Book of Love by Kelly Link--Link is apparently known for her short stories. I wish her acclaimed gift for communicating a story effectively had translated here. The book is lackluster, written with a dreamlike affect, but the story is never captivating. I never felt invested in the outcome, so instead I felt crushed by the weight of a lot of words leading to an inevitably disappointing conclusion. I have liked similar books, The Hike comes to mind, but this just doesn't cut it for me. Thumbs down.

Title: The Nazi Conspiracy: The Secret Plot to Kill Roosevelt, Stalin, and Churchill
The Wandering Earth
Review: The Wandering Earth by Cixin Liu--At times haunting and at others wry, Liu continues to capture the imagination. This book, featuring multiple short stories and one titular novella, focuses on the wonder of Earth, while properly setting the planet's place in the vastness of the universe. He considers the great foreignness of the unfamiliar by placing it in the context of what we know well, and does so skillfully, masterfully even, over and over again. It's nearly unimaginable that an author with such a (relatively) limited scope could continue to develop a sentence or two of ideas into evocative story after evocative story, but Liu succeeds. Two thumbs up.

Title: The Atlas Complex
Review: The Atlas Complex by Olivie Blake--A long time coming, this conclusion to a magical trilogy did not disappoint. The author set out to tell the stories of six individuals with the backdrop of a fantastical setting, from their perspectives, even when those perspectives were flawed. In that sense, the approach echoes an idea in the roleplaying game space--what are the lies that these characters tell themselves? The author completes these stories well, driving ever forward to a thrilling conclusion. Thumbs up.

Title: Unscripted: The Epic Battle for a Media Empire and the Redstone Family Legacy
The Burning Girls
Review: The Burning Girls by C. J. Tudor--This is a complicated book in its treatment of a variety of topics. Family, religion, legacy, and more. There are positive things about it, namely a wry and self-aware humor that permeates the story. But there are negatives too, like a fairly glib characterization of some major characters that undermines the overall tone of the book. While it's a worthwhile story, I don't know that I'll be recommending it to folks any time soon. Sideways thumb.

Title: Home Sweet Home
Review: Cool art.

Title: The Regulators
Review: The Regulators by Richard Bachman--I was primarily struck by one thing as I was reading this book, how difficult writing horror in the age of the cell phone must be. I couldn't help but think throughout the book about how many of the problems could be nearly immediately solved through ready access to a cell phone. Here, King demonstrates the early brilliance that rocketed him to preeminence in his field. King writes creepy kids better than almost anyone, and the world in this book feels lived in and viscerally real. It's a delightfully weird, though dated, romp. Thumbs up.

Title: Modern Ethics in 77 Arguments: A Stone Reader
The Big Picture: On the Origins of Life, Meaning, and the Universe Itself
A Heartbreaking Work of Staggering Genius
The Redemption of Time
Review: The Redemption of Time by Baoshu--So you thought the [REDACTED CONCLUSION] that happened at the end of The Three Body Problem was a true ending? The only thing more constant than entropy in this universe is fanfiction, actually. This fanfiction, blessed by Cixin Liu and packaged and released in China alongside his trilogy, connects dots in exactly the way one would expect from a paraquel. While parts of this book are corny, it is a full circle start, ending, and middle to an excellent trilogy. Thumbs up.

Title: First Lie Wins
Review: First Lie Wins by Ashley Elston--I have been looking forward to this book for awhile! Cat and mouse, psychological thriller, intrigue and false motives? Right up my alley. The book surpassed expectations. The plot is pretty fun--a woman is madly in love with a new man she's met and moves in with him, but he's actually just an assignment for her to commit corporate espionage on. The pace is frantic but the author never loses control of the narrative. Jump cuts through different points in time color the story until the incredible conclusion. Thumbs up.

Title: Lucky Loser: How Donald Trump Squandered His Father's Fortune and Created the Illusion of Success
The Case of the Murderous Dr. Cream: The Hunt for a Victorian Era Serial Killer
Without a Prayer: The Death of Lucas Leonard and How One Church Became a Cult
The Wonder Test
Review: The Wonder Test by Michelle Richmond--Frustratingly lackluster, this book examines an FBI agent who finds that her perfect new community has a secret. This could have been a potent skewering of the 9.9 percent and the cottage industry around elite school preparation. Instead it's not unnecessarily focused on intrigue that isn't there, with spies and diplomats thrown into the story haphazardly. Fun premise, disappointing outcome. Thumbs down.

Title: Master of Evil
Review: A dark and contemplative adventure, this book takes place in the immediate aftermath of the events of Revenge of the Sith. Vader, fully recovered from his medical issues, rises in power, and puts events into motion to both serve and undercut the Emperor. It's a carefully-considered telling of Vader's rise and struggle with power, though ultimately the outcome of the book is constrained by the canon that surrounds it, and is fairly predictable. Thumbs up.

Title: Absolution
Review: Absolution by Jeff VanderMeer--Revisiting the world of the Southern Reach/Annihilation trilogy felt like a bad idea, but this book was surprisingly good. The plot is haunting, ethereal even. There's not much more to say other than to praise the words used. Each sentence adds to the atmospheric weirdness of Area X and the tension peaks right when it needs to. Thumbs up.

Title: Chokepoint Capitalism: How Big Tech and Big Content Captured Creative Labor Markets and How We'll Win Them Back
When Genius Failed: The Rise and Fall of Long-Term Capital Management
Metallic Realms
Review: Metallic Realms by Lincoln Michel--There's a lot going on in this collection of (very) short stories. It's tough to articulate exactly what this book is, other than a love (and sometimes hate) letter to nerd culture, the publishing industry, struggling artists, and the tenuous ties of friendship. If this sounds like a Herculean task, it's compounded by the fact that this story features such a unique framing, narrated by a man who has tasked himself with assembling the collected stories of his friend's science fiction writing collective. The stories themselves deal with self-inserts in fiction, fan-fiction, even working out conflict through collaboration. But the overarching frame of a literal and metaphorical outsider tying all this together is, at equal turns, gut-wrenching and absurd in the best way. What a delight. Two thumbs up.

Title: Reimagining Capitalism in a World on Fire
Don't Pee on My Leg and Tell Me It's Raining: America's Toughest Family Court Judge Speaks Out
Retail Gangster: The Insane, Real-Life Story of Crazy Eddie
Pale Horse Rider: William Cooper, the Rise of Conspiracy, and the Fall of Trust in America
Blink: The Power of Thinking Without Thinking
How to Not Die Alone: The Surprising Science That Will Help You Find Love
Inside the Mind of BTK: The True Story Behind the Thirty-Year Hunt for the Notorious Wichita Serial Killer
God and the Gay Christian: The Biblical Case in Support of Same-Sex Relationships
Julia
Review: Julia by Sandra Newman--This retelling of 1984 from Julia's perspective is punchy yet weighty. It captures some of the focus and sobriety of Orwell while bringing in certain modern sensibilities. I think the main thing I'm taking away is the apparent inevitability of systems once they're set in motion. Thumbs up.

Title: Losing Our Religion: An Altar Call for Evangelical America
Bad Company: Private Equity and the Death of the American Dream
More Everything Forever: AI Overlords, Space Empires, and Silicon Valley's Crusade to Control the Fate of Humanity
Review: More Everything Forever by Adam Becker--I've been working on this book for a bit, and it's depressing. Getting into the thought processes of tech futurists and billionaires, often through firsthand interviews, Becker unpacks the intersection of sci-fi optimism with the downright dystopian values exhibited today. One example that stuck with me is in an examination of effective altruism, where Becker finds that the most well-connected members of the movement tend to favor fuzzy math which leads to initiatives that might have a 1/10,000 chance of massively benefiting humanity thousands of years from now versus initiatives which have an impact today. Along the way, Becker works to debunk some of the optimism around interstellar travel and some of the pessimism around AGI. I've read books about cults before, and it's surprising how similar this book, about some of the most well-connected folks in modern society, reads. Thumbs up.

Title: Mycroft and Sherlock: The Empty Birdcage
Fall: The Mysterious Life and Death of Robert Maxwell, Britain's Most Notorious Media Baron
American Radical: Inside the World of an Undercover Muslim FBI Agent
The Moment of Truth: A Novel of the Future
Atomic Habits: An Easy & Proven Way to Build Good Habits & Break Bad Ones
The Perfect Nanny
Review: The Perfect Nanny by Leila Silmani--There is little traditional  mystery in this psychological thriller. Instead, it's calm and incisive. Every description and piece of dialogue penetrates further into the lives of a family with a nanny, until the end result feels...not inevitable, but explored. While there's certainly more questions than answers in this book, it's a worthwhile journey nonetheless. Thumbs up.

Title: Such a Bad Influence
Review: Such A Bad Influence by Olivia Muenter--Simultaneously thrilling and deep, the story here explores a child/teen influencer who goes missing. The social commentary is both thoughtful and scathing, skewering both the parents who can exploit their kids as well as the audience who consumes the content. And overall, it's undergird by a dark story I thoroughly enjoyed. Thumbs up.

Title: Bright Objects
Review: Bright Objects by Ruby Todd--On its surface, this is a murder mystery, focusing on a woman whose husband tragically lost his life. But deeper still is a meditation on control and grief, obsession and anguish. Oh, and there's also maybe a cult. I'm really struck by the characterization and prose here. Inhabitants of a small Australia town are fleshed out and vibrant, and the words and descriptions themselves are lyrical, nearly poetic. The conclusion is satisfying, but the book is certainly more about the journey than the destination. Thumbs up.

Title: Good Reasonable People: The Psychology Behind America's Dangerous Divide
The Family Fang
Review: The Family Fang by Kevin Wilson--Heartwrenching and hilarious, this book is a meditation on family. It focuses on two adult children of experimental artists who consistently involved their children in uncomfortable pieces of performance art growing up. It's a slow, thoughtful book, taking time to let the characters process events as they happen. It's also quite funny. Overall, I really enjoyed it. Thumbs up.

Title: The Color of Law: A Forgotten History of How Our Government Segregated America
Review: The Color of Law by Richard Rothstein--Reading this book, I was reminded of an all-white team of high school students I recently was helping to coach, who argued to me that any form of housing discrimination laws are immoral and unconstitutional because they limit the freedom of a property owner to do what they see fit. Turns out, these students would have found fast friends in the government bureaucracy not too long ago. This is a sobering book, examining how both action and inaction on the government's part can have a disproportionate impact in perpetuating racism. Some things, particularly as they relate to my area of expertise, are pretty astonishing, including guidelines for FHA loans in effect through the 1950s and 60s that required banks to consider the "suitability" of the occupant for the neighborhood of the house they wanted to purchase. This is a powerful book about how the mundane can have outsized impact. Thumbs up.

Title: So Say We All: The Complete, Uncensored, Unauthorized Oral History of Battlestar Galactica
All This and More
Review: All This and More by Peng Shepherd--This is a good time. Think the focus on regret in The Midnight Library, changed to a voyeuristic reality TV-style focus on desire. Part timey-wimey quantum entanglement nonsense, part family drama, and part unsettling mystery. My one hesitation about this book is that I had the luxury of reading it in one fairly continuous sitting, and I can see how a reader taking it on in small servings might not enjoy it as much. As it is, this is like an adult Choose Your Own Adventure that's absolutely bursting at the seams with good vibes. Thumbs up.

Title: Superspy Science: Science, Death and Tech in the World of James Bond
Annie Bot
Review: Annie Bot by Sierra Greer--What do we want from the people around us? That question is at the core of this book about a lifelike sex robot and an owner who wants more for his robot...until he doesn't. There's some interesting concepts of autonomy and control, but in general, I wasn't all that impressed. I feel like I've read better implemented books or short stories in this vein. Sideways thumb.

Title: Rebooting AI: Building Artificial Intelligence We Can Trust
How to Take Over the World: Practical Schemes and Scientific Solutions for the Aspiring Supervillain
Poisoner in Chief: Sidney Gottlieb and the CIA Search for Mind Control
Unequal: The Maths of When Things Do and Don't Add Up
The Decagon House Murders
Review: The Decagon House Murders by Yukito Ayatsuji--Renowned for basically reviving the murder mystery genre in Japan, this book is simultaneously a love letter to the golden age of detective novels and a bold step forward. The story focuses on a murder mystery fan club that chooses to spend a weekend at the site of a notorious murder. It's written well, jumping between the travails of the mystery club and the events of the original investigation. I really appreciated the ending, as multiple genre tropes are turned on their head. Thumbs up.

Title: The Sellout
Review: The Sellout by Paul Beatty--This book purports to be a satire around the reimplementation of slavery. To be honest, I don't fully understand the hype around this book. It's shocking throughout and occasionally sentimental, but as both satire and story, it struggles. The satire, to the extent it exists, is wielded as subtly as a bowling pin to the brain, and it's tough to root for any particular character throughout. In some respects this feels like a way-too-long Onion article. Thumbs down.

Title: After World
Review: After World by Debbie Urbanski--I was expecting a fairly typical YA dystopian novel with this. The concept is simple--humanity has become largely involuntarily sterilized by a malicious (or benevolent) AI, and world governments are collaborating to create Departments of Transition to rid the world of humanity, moving everyone to a virtual cloud so that the earth can exist in peace. However, the story is told experimentally, through the lens of a different AI system assigned to craft narratives about the human experience. As the AI writes more and more about the last human on earth, it starts inserting itself into the story more and more. The result is a beautiful book, similar to World War Z in some respects such as the variety of first- and second- hand sources used to "craft" the narrative, but entirely its own thing as well. Thumbs up.

Title: I Have No Mouth and I Must Scream
Review: A surprisingly engaging collection of stories from a master of short form sci-fi writing, this book really impressed me. It's painful at times, with individual stories unresolved, but that doesn't stop the collection from doing powerful things in just a few pages.

Title: Doctors from Hell: The Horrific Account of Nazi Experiments on Humans
The Martians: The True Story of an Alien Craze That Captured Turn-Of-The-Century America
The Gate of the Feral Gods
The Highest Law in the Land: How the Unchecked Power of Sheriffs Threatens Democracy
The Hospital at the End of the World
Jennifer Government
Review: Jennifer Government by Max Barry--I don't remember where I saw this book recommended, but it was fun! If you're looking for hard-hitting satire, this fits the bill. It's about a hypercapitalistic American privatized empire. The government is neutered in favor of megacorporations that run the world. I was trying to figure out why the big tech companies weren't all that represented in the companies panned here, like McDonald's, PepsiCo, Nike, the NRA, and more. Then I realized after reading that the book was written and published in 2002. So from that metric, it's pretty solid! The book is a little too on the nose for my tastes. By the time you've read fifty pages, you get the gist of the remaining story. But it's still witty and a fun read. Thumbs up.

Title: A Man Called Ove
Review: A Man Called Ove by Frederik Backman--This book is about the wide impact one person can have. It follows a cranky old man, set in his ways and making sure every person he comes in contact with knows it. Backman takes time to explore the incidents which shaped Ove's life and how they manifest in action when a new family moves in next door. The book is sentimental and surprisingly funny, full of charm. Thumbs up.

Title: Code Dependent: Living in the Shadow of AI
Talking to Strangers: What We Should Know about the People We Don't Know
The Ride of a Lifetime: Lessons Learned from 15 Years as CEO of the Walt Disney Company
The Blue, Beautiful World
Review: The Blue, Beautiful World by Karen Lord--Undulating smoothly between genres, this book is a different kind of first contact story. It's epic in scale by some measures, yet remains focused on the relationships between characters.  It's a fun, smooth read. Thumbs up.

Title: Cults Like Us: Why Doomsday Thinking Drives America
Limitarianism: The Case Against Extreme Wealth
What I Was Doing While You Were Breeding: A Memoir
He Who Fights with Monsters, Book 1
Review: The main character is smarmy and simultaneously is a dark edgy bad boy and a goodie two-shoes. The characterization is jarring and largely unpalatable. I don't intend to continue reading these books.

Title: Doughnut Economics: Seven Ways to Think Like a 21st-Century Economist
Leave the World Behind
Review: Leave the World Behind by Rumaan Alam--Short and captivating, this book explores a terrible vacation. A family vacationing in Long Island is surprised when a couple claiming to be the AirBnB owners stops by looking for shelter. This is one of those books that had me eager for the movie, since I think the pacing struggled here. It's a compelling story about trust and family, but instead of a lurking sense of foreboding, you just get the general idea that things are weird, and things never move beyond that. With that said, I did enjoy the book and look forward to watching the movie now. Thumbs up.

Title: The Word Detective: Searching for the Meaning of It All at the Oxford English Dictionary
Model Home
Review: Model Home by Rivers Solomon--I don't believe I can describe this book as anything other than an ordeal. Off the top, I want to make clear, this book features sexual assault/child predation as a key element. With that said, this is a powerful book from the very first page. The writing is lyrical, nearly poetic, while the story itself is wrestling with a lot of different themes. Race, class, sexuality and gender, the duty of a parent to their children and vice-versa, it's all up for grabs in this book. While these subjects are certainly entwined, they each get space to breath, space to get close and personal. Often uncomfortably close, after all, this book is an ordeal. I will not forget this book any time soon. The only thing that keeps it from being rated higher for me is that the audience of people I can wholeheartedly recommend it to is small. Nevertheless, thumbs up.

Title: The Gangs of Zion: A Black Cop's Crusade in Mormon Country
Review: The Gangs of Zion by Ron Stallworth--This book is written with an occasionally corny earnestness. By the author of Black Klansman, Stallworth uses this book instead to talk about his work rooting out gang activity in 1990s Salt Lake City. He tells the story of his journey to learning more about "gangsta rap" while experiencing pushback both in and out of the police department. This is a refreshing and nuanced look at policing as it can exist. Thumbs up.

Title: The Power to Destroy: How the Antitax Movement Hijacked America
Review: The Power to Destroy: How the Antitax Movement Hijacked America by Michael Graetz--Incisive and encyclopedic, Graetz provides a comprehensive overview of the modern iteration of anti-taxation movements. He not-so-charitably starts with the end of segregated schools, and white suburban parents suddenly kicking up a fuss about state and local taxes, following that thread through Reagan's presidency. The book does a good job of getting in the weeds on motivations behind particular tax increases and decreases in the last few decades. Thumbs up.

Title: The Puzzle Master
Review: The Puzzle Master by Danielle Trussoni--I don't know why I tortured myself by going for a second helping. I think since I started with what was technically the sequel, I felt I needed to give this book a shot. I think the problems I generally had with describing how one solves a puzzle from the prior book are also at play here. Second, now that I have two books to examine, this draws very much from the Dan Brown-esque "roguishly handsome protagonist with a different woman on his arm every adventure" without the intrigue since this book is so hyper-focused on puzzles. Finally, I think something that's off-putting is the protagonist's "acquired savant syndrome", which he acquired as a "promising Midwestern football star". I can't quite pinpoint why this is something I dislike other than perhaps this being a manufactured way for your protagonist to exhibit superhuman autistic characteristics while still dealing otherwise "normally" with the rest of the world. Fool me twice, shame on me. I shan't be fooled again. Thumbs down.

Title: Five Survive
Review: Five Survive by Holly Jackson--This book begins with, among other things, a diagram mapping the interior of an RV. The proverbial and literal ride is thrilling, keeping the reader guessing from page to page. This book does a really good job of presenting a group under stress, albeit in increasingly ridiculous circumstances. Thumbs up.

Title: A First-Class Catastrophe: The Road to Black Monday, the Worst Day in Wall Street History
Think Like a Monk: Train Your Mind for Peace and Purpose Every Day
Going Clear: Scientology, Hollywood, and the Prison of Belief
The Physics of Everyday Things: The Extraordinary Science Behind an Ordinary Day
Review: The Physics of Everyday Things by James Kakalios--This book wastes no time getting into the physics of everyday things. Exactly what it says on the tin. Kakalios walks through a work travel day, pointing out how every step of the way, your day is impacted by physics. I appreciated the focus on the physics in electronics, which often "work" while being inscrutable to the common person. Specifically, I learned something new about how my remote key fob for my car works. Wow. What an exciting Monday. Thumbs up.

Title: UFO: The Inside Story of the U.S. Government's Search for Alien Life Here―and Out There
Review: UFO by Garrett M. Graff--Functionally journalistic in approach, Graff takes on the UFO controversy remain largely solidly grounded in the government's investigative approach. From legitimate coverups to as-yet unsolved mysteries, Graff presents each neutrally, perhaps even skeptically. With that said, there's no shortage of bait for UFO enthusiasts to latch onto. It's entertaining and thorough. Thumbs up.

Title: Servants of the Damned: Giant Law Firms, Donald Trump, and the Corruption of Justice
American Predator: The Hunt for the Most Meticulous Serial Killer of the 21st Century
The Credentialed Court: Inside the Cloistered, Elite Supreme Court
The Palestine Laboratory: How Israel Exports the Technology of Occupation Around the World
Review: The Palestine Laboratory by Antony Loewenstein--This is not a book about Palestine. The author is certainly empathetic toward Palestinians generally, but this is a book about Israel first and foremost, and Palestine ends up being a pretty bit player. I only bring this up because the book's thesis has to do with Israel abusing its relationship with Palestine for its own game but ends up being about Israel interacting with the world more generally in ways the author disagrees with. Not a ton of new ground is being explored here, and the author largely fails to bear out his thesis, so it leaves me wondering what we're doing here. Sideways thumb.

Title: Sister, Sinner: The Miraculous Life and Mysterious Disappearance of Aimee Semple McPherson
The Anomaly
Review: The Anomaly by Hervé Le Tellier--This book is what would happen if Manifest and Lost, black box mystery shows, met up with literary thrillers and had a polite discussion. The story contemplates a flight which, after some turbulence, lands without incident. Then the flight lands again, three months later. The philosophical, logistical, and societal concerns with having duplicates of living (and unliving) people are explored with a lot of care in this book that leaves more questions than answers. Thumbs up.

Title: Erasing History: How Fascists Rewrite the Past to Control the Future
Review: Erasing History by Jason Stanley--Short and chock-full of information, Stanley examines international and domestic lurches toward fascism, in his view, predominantly through the lens of restricting/controlling information. While the information he shares is fact-based, I'll share that I think some of the comparisons he makes between fascistic regimes abroad and nominally similar efforts in the States are in poor taste. With that said, Stanley makes a case that there is cause for concern in the areas of education and censorship, and, hey, I live in Oklahoma. He can't be that far off. Thumbs up.

Title: Circle of Hope: A Reckoning with Love, Power, and Justice in an American Church
Unit X: How the Pentagon and Silicon Valley Are Transforming the Future of War
Review: Unit X by Raj M. Shah and Christopher Kirchhoff--Weird to say, but I've never been more optimistic about the American military than after reading this book. Shah and Kirchhoff were tasked with standing up a unit in the Pentagon to remove barriers to procurement and invest in experimental startups (through POs) to help the war effort. While the perspective is biased, there's comprehensive detail of the barriers facing military procurement from the group that literally wrote the book on circumvent procurement rules. Thumbs up.

Title: The Devil and Sherlock Holmes: Tales of Murder, Madness, and Obsession
Dangerous Ideas: A Brief History of Censorship in the West, from the Ancients to Fake News
The Hank Show: How a House-Painting, Drug-Running DEA Informant Built the Machine That Rules Our Lives
Review: The Hank Show by McKenzie Funk--This is a story about Hank Asher, a drug smuggler turned data synthesizer who nearly singlehandedly constructed the modern data economy. It's also a story about the overreach of today's surveillance state. Funk convincingly, though briefly, argues that the United States has become the tech dystopia we feared from places like China or Germany and are bafflingly comfortable with this setup because of the mask this data collection wears. We are comfortable with T-Mobile collecting (and selling) our data for use by law enforcement or ad targeting in the same way that we allow Amazon or State Farm or Taco Bell or 7-Eleven to track and build profiles on us. And it all started with a cocaine smuggler...thumbs up.

Title: Unorthodox: The Scandalous Rejection of My Hasidic Roots
Agent to the Stars
Review: Agent to the Stars by John Scalzi--This is a fast-paced, delightfully witty book about an agent representing some of the relatively mediocre Hollywood stars who is asked by aliens to represent them as they introduce themselves to humanity. I enjoyed the skewering of the industry as a whole, while the story itself is filled with delightful mishaps and good clean fun. Thumbs up.

Title: The Road to Freedom: Economics and the Good Society
How UFOs Conquered the World: The History of a Modern Myth
Cultural Identity and the Purposes of God: A Biblical Theology of Ethnicity, Nationality, and Race
Review: Cultural Identity and the Purposes of God by Steven Bryan--Rich with anecdotes and textual analysis of Scripture, this book provides an introduction to a schema of reconciling diversity and faith. The book itself is certainly thought-provoking, but the author interprets some stories in the Bible in a fairly unique way that happens to line up with his book's thesis. To be clear, I'm not contesting the overall message of the book, that cultural Identity and diversity are features of God's plan for humanity as outlined in Scripture, I simply think that occasionally the wrong equations are used to get to the right answer. Color is provided in the author's experiences teaching in Ethiopia for decades and it is clear that he has wrestled with the questions (and answers) raised here. Thumbs up.

Title: Marvel 1602
Contributors: Andy Kubert, Richard Isanove, Todd Klein, Peter Sanderson

Title: Dreyer's English: An Utterly Correct Guide to Clarity and Style
Foolproof: Why Misinformation Infects Our Minds and How to Build Immunity
How to Win Friends and Influence Enemies: Deliver Winning Conservative Arguments Against Mainstream Media
Super Pumped: The Battle for Uber
Everything is Obvious: Once You Know the Answer
Review: Everything is Obvious by Duncan Watts--This book is refreshing in what it doesn't know. That is, while a lot of different topics get brought up, Watts is refreshing in the way he lays out what we have yet to learn in social sciences. That broad array of topics includes prediction markets, political beliefs in friend groups, data and advertising, and justice when a choice's impact is disproportionately large compared to its in-the-moment evaluation. Throughout, there's a good exploration of biases and patterns that it's easy to fall into. Thumbs up.

Title: Pickleball For Dummies
Authors: Reine Steel, Diana Landau, Carl Landau, Mo Nard

Title: The House in the Cerulean Sea
Review: The House in the Cerulean Sea by TJ Klune--Charming and delightful, the best way I could describe this book to someone who hadn't read it is like an upbeat magical fantasy Series of Unfortunate Events from the perspective of Mr. Poe. I won't spoil much here because the journey is all of the fun, but this is a feel-good book full of precocious children, slow-burn romance (sometimes literally), and a man who's absolutely devoted to rules and regulations. I'm really impressed. Thumbs up.

Title: City of Rivals: Restoring the Glorious Mess of American Democracy
Review: City of Rivals by Jason Grumet--It feels like a good week to read about nothing being done in DC. This book feels almost adrift in time, written about a decade ago and very much of a different political era. Proposed reforms range from absurdly idealistic to surprisingly practical, getting in the weeds on how Congress could be operating better. With that said, the fact that this book was written before the ousters of folks like Liz Cheney and Kevin McCarthy and before the events of January 6th kind of haunts the pages. It's a specter looking over robust discussion of streamlining Senate confirmation hearings. I don't know how much this book can possibly offer to the political climate of today, but it's well-conceived, even if it defends lobbyists too much for my liking. Thumbs up.

Title: Mad Honey
Review: This book really got to me. A murder mystery/legal thriller, this book follows a woman whose son, a star athlete, is accused of a brutal murder of his loving girlfriend. The story unfolds from two different perspectives, and is brilliantly done. Often when I've read books from different perspectives, the characters are wholly different, allowing them each to have distinct voices. This book has two very similar protagonists, yet manages to have wholly separate voices throughout. I don't know if I've read a book more filled with empathetic realism than this one, and the story is at a simmer the whole time, always one tablespoon of honey away from an explosion. There's some really masterful stuff being done with time and perspective in this book as well, but elaborating wouldn't bee prudent. Two thumbs up.

Title: How Not to Be Wrong: The Power of Mathematical Thinking
Review: How Not To Be Wrong by Jordan Ellenberg--About as cozy as a math book can get, this book focuses on logic and how folks think about the world from a mathematical perspective. He looks at baseball, the lottery, replicability in experiments, whether or not God exists, Antonin Scalia, and more. Good book, easy to understand, no complaints. Thumbs up.

Title: Meme Wars: The Untold Story of the Online Battles Upending Democracy in America
Authors: Brian Friedberg, Emily Dreyfuss, Joan Donovan

Title: Utopia for Realists: How We Can Build the Ideal World
Review: Utopia for Realists by Rutger Bregman--This book is small and packs a punch. Bregman makes a convincing argument in favor of a shorter workweek, universal basic income, and more, all in favor of a scientifically-supported utopia. I think he makes compelling arguments, however, it's where he advocates for wholly open borders that he starts to lose me. He brushes past arguments against open borders, and his explanation about the economic impacts runs starkly counter to his own arguments in favor of UBI. With that said, it's a pragmatic look at the 21st century and the future of work (hopefully?). Thumbs up.

Title: Team of Rivals: The Political Genius of Abraham Lincoln
Red Pill, Blue Pill: How to Counteract the Conspiracy Theories That Are Killing Us
The Trading Game
Review: The Trading Game by Gary Stevenson--This is written quite well. Stevenson was a successful trader in the wake of the financial crisis and claims to have made his substantial wealth at Citibank by betting on growing inequality post-crisis. More interesting than his trading strategy, however, is the personalities in the trading pit, brought to life by surprisingly rich prose. This really is a dark book. I had to take a break at one point just processing what was going on. The people are dreadful to each other, author included. Stevenson effectively skewers the industry he grew his wealth in, while at the end, advocating for a better future. It's a haunting read. Thumbs up.

Title: Freakonomics: A Rogue Economist Explores the Hidden Side of Everything
Authors: Steven D. Levitt, Stephen J. Dubner

Title: The Janus Point: A New Theory of Time
Review: The Janus Point by Julian Barbour--In-depth and novel, this book proposes a somewhat new theory of the universe's origin and, indeed, of time itself. The book is relatively simple to follow and isn't pedantic. Barbour also completes a comprehensive evaluation of the history of scientific dialogue around his area of expertise. Thumbs up.

Title: One Perfect Couple
Review: One Perfect Couple by Ruth Ware--It's Agatha Christie for the era of reality TV. A couple in a long-term relationship sign up for a dating competition against other couples where folks will be eliminated one at a time. The story proceeds in a very fun way, with a reveal I didn't see coming. Death and secrets and romance abound in this book that's a fun satire of the reality TV phenomenon. Thumbs up.

Title: The Offset
Review: The Offset by Natasha Calder and Emma Szewczak--Dystopia with little redeeming it, this book follows a series of relatively unlikable characters living in the age of the Offset, where anyone who chooses to have a child must face the consequences when the child turns 18--the child must choose a parent to die in a public execution. I'm not sure the unexpected twist in this story does enough to save the book. I'm not overly impressed, but I can imagine some folks who might like the book. Sideways thumb.

Title: Babel
Review: Babel by R. F. Kuang--I have been postponing this for far too long. This story blends a surprisingly artful metaphor for empire with full, rich characters and a world that is real and gritty and messy. This is a speculative fiction work that follows a young man attending Oxford as a novice translator in a world where translation powers everything. Kuang takes time to flesh out and truly reckon with the consequences of the world she's built while making it feel grounded in moments of pure delight and heartbreak. I felt like I had a gut punch while I was deplaning and came to a particular part of the story that I won't soon forget. I do not think everyone should read this book, but everyone who wants to read it should read it immediately. Two thumbs up.

Title: Wake Up and Open Your Eyes
The Myth of Persecution: How Early Christians Invented a Story of Martyrdom
Review: The Myth of Persecution by Candida Moss--Exhaustively researched and surprisingly nuanced, this book explores the history of persecution in the early church. Moss visits the early Christian church as it begins to develop its own mythology, from beleaguered saints to the glorification of martyrs. Artificial martyrdom and the exaggeration of persecution certainly have parallels to the modern day, and Moss makes the argument that the American church's obsession with Christianity being persecuted ends up drawing well-deserved attention from actual atrocities globally. Thumbs up.

Title: America's Deadliest Election: The Cautionary Tale of the Most Violent Election in American History
Review: America's Deadliest Election by Dana Bash--I didn't come into this book expecting much, but was left surprised by this fascinating and likely little-known story. It details the 1872 gubernatorial election in Louisiana and the personalities behind it which spurred violence, tax strikes, and discontent. The parallels to the modern day are clear, particularly in the legal battles where one potential governor almost-but-not-quite directly ordered murders and enacted his own rules in pursuit of his own political aims. It's both a sobering and deeply impactful read, particularly in its analysis of the outcomes which still resonate today. Thumbs up.

Title: Bible Nation: The United States of Hobby Lobby
Authors: Joel S. Baden, Candida R. Moss
Review: Bible Nation by Candida Moss and Joel Baden--Certainly physically close to home for me, if nothing else, this book reviews the Green family, the billionaires who founded and run Hobby Lobby. The book dives deep into the Greens' attempts to influence Oklahoma education on the Bible as well as broader scholarship on the origins of Scripture. While it's a solid book, it's a little frustrating that the Museum of the Bible is a centerpiece of the narrative yet the authors did not visit the Museum until after the book was written and was in preproduction. It feels like the authors were pressed to a deadline that it wasn't reasonable for them to reach. However, the book is informative. Thumbs up.

Title: Data and Goliath: The Hidden Battles to Collect Your Data and Control Your World
Review: Data and Goliath by Bruce Schneier--Schneier quickly and adeptly dives into the data we leak out every day and the corporate and government vacuums that suck it all up for their own purposes. This is the most strident that I've seen Schneier write, he sees data privacy as the singular issue we are failing at, with what he views as potentially catastrophic consequences. The reforms he proposed are similarly revolutionary (and likely so far out of reach as to be classified as fantasy). He wants, for example, the NSA to disclose zero-day exploits it identifies rather than exploiting them for their own purposes. It's a well-meaning, but ultimately sad book. Thumbs up.

Title: Unruly: A History of England's Kings and Queens
Review: Unruly by David Mitchell--What a delight. This primer on British royalty is written in Mitchell's signature comedic voice and is a quick and dirty (often dirty) look at the kings and queens of Britain. From children manipulated to govern to queens that get unfairly maligned, this book really has it all. Thumbs up.

Title: Lives of the Stoics: The Art of Living from Zeno to Marcus Aurelius
MoneyGPT: AI and the Threat to the Global Economy
Reboot
Review: Reboot by Justin Taylor--Surreal and hilarious, this book dives deep (sometimes too deep) into fan culture in service of this story about a cult favorite show that might be getting a reboot. The story is extremely online, sometimes in ways that are uncomfortably close to home.  The ride is a blast, and the book is more insightful than I would have expected. Thumbs up.

Title: The Tenant
Review: The Tenant by Frieda McFadden--i think this book most clearly demonstrated weaknesses in McFadden's writing. In addition to being fairly predictable for a thriller, this is the first book of hers that I've read written from a man's perspective. The man is extremely poorly written, in a way not all that dissimilar from some critiques of male authors writing characters who are women. I didn't love this and honestly it was a bit of a struggle to finish. Thumbs down.

Title: Mindhunter: Inside the FBI's Elite Serial Crime Unit
Batman: The Long Halloween
Contributors: Tim Sale, Gregory Wright, Richard Starkings

Title: Deep Utopia: Life and Meaning in a Solved World
Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind
Chip War: The Fight for the World's Most Critical Technology
Raw Deal: Hidden Corruption, Corporate Greed, and the Fight for the Future of Meat
The Silence Project
Review: The Silence Project by Carole Hailey--I have some mixed feelings about this book. It was a StoryGraph recommendation, which continue to be very solid, and as it's a UK published book, I had trouble finding it anywhere in the US, eventually getting it from Amazon. The book is, among other things, a commentary on misogyny and class, a veiled critique of Margaret Sanger (founder of Planned Parenthood), and an examination of a corrupted purpose, that is, what do you do when your life's work turns out to not accomplish the mission you thought it did. The book is piercing and incisive, written from the perspective of a woman whose mother starts a movement that fundamentally reshapes the world. It's somewhat similar to books like Vox (Christina Dalcher), The Power (Naomi Alderman), and The Grace Year (Kim Liggett), but feels more grounded than the others in a really powerful way. Two thumbs up.

Title: The Ministry for the Future
Review: The Ministry For The Future by Kim Stanley Robinson--Methodically pragmatic yet unwavering in its optimism, this work of fiction could be ripped from tomorrow's headlines. While the story it tracks is extremely close to home, it's clear that the author did immense research to make this book a reality. The narrative follows a hypothetical Ministry for the Future under the United Nations, but the interstitials which break up the story give it strength, as the author and other characters ponder what this all means for us in the current moment. In some ways, it really is a beautiful book, it's clear the author intends the story to be nonfiction as fiction. Two thumbs up.

Title: We the People: A History of the U.S. Constitution
Satan's Diary
Review: Satan's Diary by Nicholas Ponticello--There are areas of excellence in this book. The author fleshes out the characters in this retelling of Satan's "fall" in a way that is compelling and worth visiting. However, some of the story decisions can be baffling. When you're jumping forward for broad swaths of time in human history, the places you do step in need to be justified. With that said, this can be compared to books like Song of Achilles in that it tries to recontextualize a well known historical relationship between God and Satan. In that aim, I think the book partially succeeds, though I don't think it sticks the landing. The book's summary talks about angels with iPads and bringing a modern spin to a heavenly story. The story provides that...in the epilogue. I don't think it lived up to my expectations, though there are some touching parts. I received an advance review copy of the book, and if you want to see how much you agree with my analysis, check it out when it's published in July. Sideways thumbs.

Title: The Watchers
Review: The Watchers by A.M. Shine--Good snow day read. This book is basically a dark folk tale about creatures and places beyond human comprehension. The story successfully conveys loneliness and isolation, but perhaps deeper is a metaphor for being observed but not seen. Good book. Thumbs up.

Title: Gangsters of Capitalism: Smedley Butler, the Marines, and the Making and Breaking of America's Empire
Review: Gangsters of Capitalism by Jonathan Katz--Globe-spanning in scope, the author travels near and far to learn about American imperialism more than a century ago. He does so by profiling one significant general, Smedley Butler, who is most intriguing in his late-in-life turn toward criticizing the US government's efforts abroad. Some of the most poignant parts of this book come from the author's experiences in locations that so clearly are still impacted by US involvement decades later. The book is thoughtful and insightful, a potent combination. Thumbs up.

Title: Spellbound: How Charisma Shaped American History from the Puritans to Donald Trump
Stories Are Weapons: Psychological Warfare and the American Mind
Review: Stories are Weapons by Annalee Newitz--I wasn't expecting too much from this, but it still managed to surprise me. The book is about how narratives are the battlegrounds of the past and present, insightfully examining fights over education, comics, and tribal assimilation. This is a very current book and it's also very hopeful. Newitz believes wholeheartedly in the power of stories to shape the world, even though some domestic and foreign actors would prefer to propagate "feel-bad" stories. Thumbs up.

Title: Gregor and the Curse of the Warmbloods
Bad Data: Why We Measure the Wrong Things and Often Miss the Metrics That Matter
Sandworm: A New Era of Cyberwar and the Hunt for the Kremlin's Most Dangerous Hackers
Remote Control
Review: Remote Control by Nnedi Okorafor--I think reading this today, of all days, was a more meaningful choice than normal. It's short but complex, nearly a folk tale in its own right, or at the very least, a story of stories. It follows a young woman who comes across a power whose origin eludes her. The story touches on so much in a short time frame, from power wielded recklessly to a technology wielded maliciously. In the end, though, Okorafor brings it home to humanity and community, two concepts which one doesn't need a folk tale to remember on the sixth of November. Thumbs up.

Title: Built: The Hidden Stories Behind Our Structures
Useful Delusions: The Power and Paradox of the Self-Deceiving Brain
Snakes in Suits: When Psychopaths Go to Work
The Cuckoo's Egg: Tracking a Spy Through the Maze of Computer Espionage
Addiction by Design: Machine Gambling in Las Vegas
Review: Addiction By Design by Natasha Dow Schüll--This book is a fascinating look from an anthropological perspective on solo gambling using electronic machines. Schüll systematically outlines the systems, both internal to the machine and external in casinos themselves, which keep folks coming back for more, and, Schüll argues, prey on particularly vulnerable populations. She speaks with industry insiders and Gamblers Anonymous meetings which all express extreme reservations at the harms being done, but the underlying profit motive appears inescapable. It's a firm outline of the problem. Thumbs up.

Title: Perfect Little World
Review: Perfect Little World by Kevin Wilson--I have friends whom I cherish deeply that don't enjoy books where nothing happens. This is one of those books. For my part, however, this was a really poignant story. It focuses on a young woman who is impregnated by her high school teacher and receives an invitation to raise her child in a comprehensive research study. A lot of themes come up here that are touched on and not fully explored, from determinism to self-harm, from found family to the idea of contentment. There's not a lot of action supporting this plot, but in some crucial ways it's a beautiful snapshot of life. Thumbs up.

Title: Outlive: The Science and Art of Longevity
This Way to the Universe: A Theoretical Physicist's Journey to the Edge of Reality
Beautyland
Review: Beautyland by Marie Helene-Bertino--This book taps into a surprisingly human feeling, that of feeling alien. It's also about memory and how the stories we tell ourselves end up defining us. I think the way the story is crafted is beautiful and some paragraphs, focused on viewing humanity from the outside looking in, remain extremely memorable. This is wonderful slow-burn science fiction. Thumbs up.

Title: Remarkably Bright Creatures
Review: Remarkably Bright Creatures by Shelby Van Pelt--Charming and smart, this book reminds me most of A Man Called Ove. It follows a woman, an octopus, and a life worth living, and I enjoyed the route taken to get there. There's not a ton of meat to this book, but sometimes the real meat is the friends we made along the way. Thumbs up.

Title: The Value of Everything: Making and Taking in the Global Economy
Review: The Value of Everything by Mariana Mazzucato--This book is not for the faint of heart. It is by an economist, written for economists. But there's some great stuff here. The book starts by asking the reader to consider what value is--and what it isn't. When a bank makes an interest spread on loans versus deposits, is that value created for society? How does it differ from the value generated by stay-at-home spouses doing work around the house? Or from that generated by Factory Freddy, who goes to work assembling widgets every day? Or from what's generated by the United States Postal Service, a government-run semi-private entity? The extent to which any of these can or should be considered "value", particularly in a Gross Domestic Product sense, is highly debated, and that debate is fleshed out in this book in sometimes exhausting detail. With that said, the book has one of the best frameworks for thinking about public value for public good that I've seen. Read on, my friends, but only if you're ready to learn. Thumbs up.

Title: More Numbers Every Day: How Data, Stats, and Figures Control Our Lives and How to Set Ourselves Free
White Too Long: The Legacy of White Supremacy in American Christianity
The Death of Mrs. Westaway
Review: The Death of Mrs. Westaway by Ruth Ware--The initial setup is familiar. A young woman is invited to a family estate for the reading of a will for someone she has no connection to. But the outcome of the story is superb. Ware has complete mastery of creating foreboding and resonant thrillers, and her skills are on full display here. The book's running theme of tarot, of choices and interpretation and destiny, comes together intricately. Two thumbs up.

Title: Her Honor: My Life on the Bench...What Works, What's Broken, and How to Change It
The Man in the High Castle
Another Now: Dispatches from an Alternative Present
Review: Another Now by Yanis Varoufakis--While the idea of economic treatise/philosophy dissertation as fiction, science fiction at that, is interesting, I'm not sure it works well here. Varoufakis details a fictional other world where, due to the 2008 financial crisis, the world instead progresses into a modified collectivistic social capitalism. Much of the book, then, is just explaining how the system works and why it works well. I can respect the author' credentials immensely, but I think much of how he thinks about the current state of banking is so flawed as to be irredeemable, even if there's significant elements of his proposal that sound appealing. He also uses a modified version of the land value tax,l which somehow is even more riddled with problems than what I've seen of the original. Sideways thumb.

Title: Math-ish: Finding Creativity, Diversity, and Meaning in Mathematics
Review: Math-ish by Jo Boaler--Perhaps more intended for a teaching audience, this book aims to educate the reader on how to, well, educate. Focusing on math, Boaler walks the reader through meta cognitive techniques that she claims are proven to better teach students of all ages. While the subject matter is not necessarily my speed, the concept of thinking through things differently and truly interrogating why you're doing what you're doing is applicable to anyone. Thumbs up.

Title: Trust Exercise
Review: Trust Exercise by Susan Choi--We often delude ourselves into our truth. From recollections that aren't quite right to manufactured memories that soften a blow--or make it firmer--we live a life where truth, where narratives in general, are messy. Where the only consensus we can come to is flawed at best and manipulative at worst. That's the dynamic this book tries to get at, telling the story of a young woman who attends an elite performing arts school. I really enjoyed the way this was told, the novel approach is enhanced by realistic characters in a world that feels lived in, as haphazard as that can be. Thumbs up.

Title: The Brothers Hawthorne
Review: The Brothers Hawthorne by Jennifer Lynn Barnes--Look, I loved the initial Inheritance Games trilogy. I think the author is great, the story was well-crafted, a true delight with a fascinating premise. As this book demonstrates, that premise is rippling with potential for further exploration. Unfortunately, this follow up feels a bit like a lazy cash grab. Two stories both end up intersecting at exactly the point that requires more books. What a coincidence. I'll read future books, and I do think this expanded the universe of a series I liked, but the titular brothers felt like caricatures of the characters from the initial trilogy. Sideways thumb.

Title: Who Is Government?: The Untold Story of Public Service
Contributors: Sarah Vowell, John Lanchester, Dave Eggers, Geraldine Brooks, W. Kamau Bell, Casey Cep

Title: Black Pill: How I Witnessed the Darkest Corners of the Internet Come to Life, Poison Society, and Capture American Politics
Review: Black Pill by Elle Reeve--I've been fascinated by the alt right for awhile. The roots of that fascination are fairly close to home and have resulted in my reading quite a few books and taking in truly hundreds of hours (as a conservative estimate) of content diving into the conspiracies, strategies, and meming that undergirds QAnon, white nationalism, and similar movements. This book stands out in that space. It accepts as a given that folks reading it will have some idea of how the Internet works and memes spread and instead offers an unexpected look at the personalities behind the movements. One important thing this book does well is dive into the power struggle in the alt right, Richard Spencer's belittling of QAnon supporters, Unite the Right's philosophical disagreements with the admin of 8chan, and so on. Tonally, the story is told with a simultaneous deep dark humor and a push to not take these movements for granted. Two thumbs up.

Title: The Bright Sword
Review: The Bright Sword by Lev Grossman--Soaring and thrilling, this is a retelling, or perhaps just a telling, of King Arthur's court. The scene opens a few hours after the death of King Arthur and finds lesser-known members of his court trying to pick up the pieces. Knowing Arthurian legend is not a necessity, but it certainly helps. At its core, though, this book is about broken people trying to bring order and hope to a broken world, and that core message resonates whether you are intimately familiar with Mordred or not. Thumbs up.

Title: Limitless: The Federal Reserve Takes on a New Age of Crisis
Victim
Review: Victim by Andrew Boryga--Damning and scathing, this book is in some ways a send-up of "trauma porn" or what some might derisively call "victimhood Olympics". There's a dark cruelty in the story, from a young man taught to say whatever he needs to say in order to move ahead in the world to a man who simply wants to move on from his past instead of dwell in it. There's also good commentary on who gets to construct the stories we see and the perverse incentive structures at play with engagement or rage baiting. It's a clean, thought-provoking story. Thumbs up.

Title: Children of the State: Stories of Survival and Hope in the Juvenile Justice System
Conspirituality: How New Age Conspiracy Theories Became a Public Health Threat
Authors: Matthew Remski, Julian Walker, Derek Beres

Title: Lovely One
Review: Lovely One by Ketanji Brown Jackson--Rippling with hope and a clear-eyed look at society today, this book details the life of Justice Jackson. It's a powerful story, and the Justice's religious background comes into play more often than I expected. There's a lot of healthy introspection present here, as Justice Jackson explores why small moments can define us and speaks to the power of community to shape positive outcomes. Thumbs up.

Title: Project Hail Mary
Review: Project Hail Mary by Andy Weir--It's tough to find a more celebrated book out there. I ordinarily have an ardent skepticism of celebrated books, but this one is so gosh darn lovable. There's a clear love of science itself throughout the book, and the story is full of passion, hope, and humanity. This book has one of my favorite postscripts that I've read. Two thumbs up.

Title: The Empusium
Review: The Empusium by Olga Tokarczuk--Inviting to begin, this book follows a young man sent to recover from tuberculosis in the mountains. He gets more than he bargained for in this folk horror story. The dread unfolds in a matter of fact way. Mysterious happenings are laid bare on the page, and the cycle continues. It's almost chilling because of its banality. Thumbs up.

Title: One by One
Review: One By One by Ruth Ware--Truly delightful, this book follows the executives of a hip tech startup as they attend a swanky ski chalet for a corporate retreat. Things devolve from there, but the story is told really well. I found this to be much more predictable than other Ware books, but that didn't keep me from thoroughly enjoying it, particularly as this book also had a side of societal commentary. Thumbs up.

Title: Phenomena: The Secret History of the U.S. Government's Investigations into Extrasensory Perception and Psychokinesis
Westworld and Philosophy: If You Go Looking for the Truth, Get the Whole Thing
Authors: James B. South, William Irwin, Kimberly S. Engels

Title: The Courage to Be Free: Florida's Blueprint for America's Revival
The Vacation
Review: The Vacation by John Marrs--Thrilling and perspective-shifting, this book explores the lives of people brought together (mostly) by chance at a Venice Beach hostel. It's unique among the works I've read by Marrs because there's no underlying technology element, just people with secrets to hide interacting as those secrets come back to haunt them. I really enjoyed this one, couldn't wait to read every subsequent page. Thumbs up.

Title: Be Ready When the Luck Happens
Review: Be Ready When The Luck Happens by Ina Garten--Charming and intimate, this book follows the Barefoot Contessa in her own words from the start of her career to the modern day. The book is interspersed with recipes, anecdotes, and pictures that color the rich story. Much like her popular show, this book felt like the reader is invited to pull up a chair and participate in the story. It's casual, it's frank, it's wholesome. Thumbs up.

Title: Algospeak: How Social Media Is Transforming the Future of Language
Review: Algospeak by Adam Aleksic--Fascinating and important, this book by a linguistics-loving TikTok creator aims to explain everything you never considered about the omnipresent algorithm and its influence on language. From appropriation to Deaf creators to the Flanderization of individuals and identity, every page of this short book drips with knowledge and insight. It's rare that I read a nonfiction book where every chapter brings something thought-provoking to the table, but Aleksic really cooked here. At its height, this book explores conformity through the lens of linguistics, studying how otherwise niche turns of phrase seep into the "chronically offline" culture. I thoroughly enjoyed this and would recommend it to anyone intrigued or bewildered by the attentionmaxxing algorithm. Two thumbs up.

Title: I Will Teach You to Be Rich: No Guilt. No Excuses. No BS. Just a 6-Week Program That Works
The Mysterious Case of the Alperton Angels
Review: The Mysterious Case of the Alperton Angels by Janice Hallett--This fictional account of true crime research is riveting. Told via a patchwork of Whatsapp messages, interview transcripts, and email responses, the story unfolds in a way that leaves you guessing until the end. What isn't said in this book is often as noticeable as what is, and the motives for major characters are cohesive in retrospect. Thumbs up.

Title: Tired of Winning: Donald Trump and the End of the Grand Old Party
Review: Tired of Winning by Jonathan Karl--I think it's easy to become numb to the events of the last two administrations. We talk about COVID bringing about a "new normal", but it's underrated how new our political normal has become in the last two decades or so. This book approaches the phenomenon of former President Trump from the perspective of his relationship to the party, to the point of subsuming it. While much has been reported already before, some anecdotes are still truly shocking. The RNC paid (and continues to pay) millions of dollars to former President Trump because of conversations he had with Ronna McDaniel immediately upon leaving office. The party effectively sold itself, and has now cannibalized itself, molding itself more in the Great Leader's image. This is basically unprecedented in American history, and that's before you get to the former President's tenuous grasp on reality. The book is sad, it's shocking, and its reporting should be (and is) clearly and unequivocally disqualifying. Yet none of it matters. This is just another new day in American life. Thumbs up.

Title: The Future of Humanity: Terraforming Mars, Interstellar Travel, Immortality and Our Destiny Beyond Earth
The Club
Review: The Club by Ellery Lloyd--I know folks have raved about this book, but I couldn't quite get into it. I think there's a few factors going into that. The shift in perspectives across this book isn't handled all that well, with confusing jumps and some characters who are similar. I also didn't love the concept of the book to start with, but it was a psychological thriller so I gave it a chance. Made it to the end, but it was one of those books that stopped me reading for a bit because I was dreading getting through it. Sideways thumb.

Title: We Used to Live Here
Review: We Used To Live Here by Marcus Kliewer--Extremely unsettling, this thriller/horror book follows a couple who's just moved into an old house when the previous occupants stop by asking to take a look. What follows is both predictable and chilling. The vibes are immaculate, and reading this in a fully lit room during the day is a must. It's a creepypasta meets King mixed with a little bit of Parasite. Thumbs up.

Title: Episode Thirteen
Review: Episode Thirteen by Craig DiLouie--Terrifying in parts, particularly at the end, this book follows a reality TV crew trying to capture the paranormal on camera. There's so much more than just raw horror here as the story is captured via diary snippets, video transcripts, text exchanges, and more. The leap from "this is weird" to "sheer terror" is slow and steady, and the plot is skillfully laid out. Thumbs up.

Title: Corruptible: Who Gets Power and How It Changes Us
Review: Corruptible by Brian Klaas--Here, Klaas looks at the nature of power itself and of those who are drawn to it. By interviewing cult leaders, government officials, and CEOs, and drawing on scientific analysis, he puts together a framework of maintaining power well and selecting good (or at least, avoiding selecting bad) leaders. It's a straightforward but compelling book. Thumbs up.

Title: Cultish: The Language of Fanaticism
Review: Cultish by Amanda Montell--Fascinating and perceptive, this book explores the role of language across cults of all kinds. Scientology, Peloton, Mary Kay, Jonestown, and QAnon are all fair game for this exploration of how the words we use both unite and divide us. The content is sensitive and even amusing. Thumbs up.

Title: A Fever in the Heartland: The Ku Klux Klan's Plot to Take Over America, and the Woman Who Stopped Them
Review: A Fever in the Heartland by Timothy Egan--Researched well, this is the type of narrative nonfiction that cuts deep. By relaying stories of individuals rather than a full recounting of a nationwide movement, Egan is able to fully communicate the psychological and physical torture, up to and frequently including murder, wrought by the Ku Klux Klan across the country. He focuses on a particular predatory con man in the Midwest who eventually gets a reckoning because of the bravery of a little known woman. There were so many quotes I could highlight, I've attached one here for convenience. Egan, to his credit, does not draw direct parallels to today, they'd likely be in poor taste. However, as one reads this accounting of a loyalist political movement taking steps to install some of their own at every level of government, with core ideals that the sanctity of the family is being ruined by newfound American diversity, that the immigrants coming in are expressly coming from the "wrong part" of the world and thus can't live in accordance with American values, that the theory of evolution being taught in schools is a great affront to children and some heinous actions are justified to protect the American dream, that temperance and particular sexual virtues should be enforced either by governmental edict or by a vigilante force "exposing" the perversions of society...it really makes you think. Two thumbs up.

Title: Ours Was the Shining Future: The Story of the American Dream
Resistance (at All Costs): How Trump Haters Are Breaking America
A True Free Market: Conversations on Gaining Liberty and Justice Through Economics
Shape: The Hidden Geometry of Information, Biology, Strategy, Democracy, and Everything Else
Review: Shape by Jordan Ellenberg--It's almost always delightful to see someone take their knowledge and passion for a subject and share it with the world. Shape does this in spades, taking geometry and finding the joy of it in both the everyday and the complex. Ellenberg begins by walking through the satisfaction of knowing something is true through proofs, then embarks on a whirlwind world tour of checkers, COVID-19, elections, mosquitos, machine learning, Survivor, straws, and much much more. It's inviting, entertaining, and educational. Two thumbs up.

Title: The Last Murder at the End of the World
Review: The Last Murder At The End of the World by Stuart Turton--This book, written by the author of The 7 1/2 Deaths of Evelyn Hardcastle, feels both familiar and strange. It takes a locked room murder mystery to the ends of the earth, taking place entirely on an island holding the last bastion of humanity. Free will and ethics play a big part in the story here, and the characters are complete and spark empathy. I really enjoyed this, and am excited to see whatever this author puts out next. Two thumbs up.

Title: False Alarm: How Climate Change Panic Costs Us Trillions, Hurts the Poor, and Fails to Fix the Planet
Creation Lake
Review: Creation Lake by Rachel Kushner--Really artfully done, this story starts with a woman assigned to infiltrate a French commune. The narrative is punctuated by meditations on life itself through the lens of the mysterious commune leader, and the way the story unfolds is impressive. I enjoyed both reading and reading into this book. Thumbs up.

Title: The Divider: Trump in the White House, 2017-2021
Trust and Consequences (Worth the Candle, Book 2)
Moving the Needle: What Tight Labor Markets Do for the Poor
Authors: Katherine S. Newman, Elisabeth S Jacobs

Title: The Return of Great Powers: Russia, China, and the Next World War
Review: The Return of Great Powers by Jim Sciutto--In clear terms, Sciutto describes the current state of the world. He examines the three great powers in the world and their capabilities, goals, and probable conflicts going forward. This book combines on-the-ground reporting with compelling interviews at the highest levels of government in the United States, Germany, Taiwan, and Ukraine. It's exhaustively sourced while being a compelling story, not just of today, but of tomorrow. Thumbs up.

Title: Under the Whispering Door
Review: Under The Whispering Door by TJ Klune--Delightfully wholesome, this book follows a dead man. That is, a man who seems to have lived a fairly dreadful existence before dying. Upon his death, he discovers a strange new world he becomes stuck in. Look, this formula is very similar to Klune's other book I have read, but it's still impactful. Resonant, fun, a good lunchtime read. Thumbs up.

Title: The Devil and the Dark Water
Breath: The New Science of a Lost Art
Surprise, Kill, Vanish: The Secret History of CIA Paramilitary Armies, Operators, and Assassins
The Retreat
Review: The Retreat by Sarah Pearse--I liked the first book, but I don't think the main character could support another book without substantive changes. Unfortunately, this book doesn't make those changes. The Retreat retreads the Sanatorium, even following some of the same character "development" for the main character. It's lackluster, a sequel in search of innovation but coming up short. Sideways thumb.

Title: Loserthink: How Untrained Brains Are Ruining America
The Fourth Turning Is Here: What the Seasons of History Tell Us about How and When This Crisis Will End
Eruption
Review: Eruption by James Patterson and Michael Crichton--This feels  like the literary equivalent of click bait. One of the most prolific and notable authors of our time meets a lost manuscript of one of the best thriller writers of all time. What could go wrong? The answer is everything. Written in the signature Patterson style (relentless, often one-page chapters with no reason for their separation), and a Crichton story that would have been lackluster even if it had been appropriately fleshed out, this book is phoned in and sad. Little characterization, poor plotting, maybe the only upside is the depiction of the volcano as a monster throughout, looming over the face of the book. Patterson should stick to what he knows--torturing librarians who have to alphabetize shelf after shelf of his works. Thumbs down.

Title: All the Knowledge in the World: The Extraordinary History of the Encyclopedia
The Book of Strange New Things
Blowback: A Warning to Save Democracy From the Next Trump
A People's History of the United States
This Time Is Different: Eight Centuries of Financial Folly
Authors: Kenneth S. Rogoff, Carmen M. Reinhart

Title: In the Valleys of the Noble Beyond: In Search of the Sasquatch
Unabridged: The Thrill of and Threat to the Modern Dictionary
So Good They Can't Ignore You: Why Skills Trump Passion in the Quest for Work You Love
Review: So Good They Can't Ignore You by Cal Newport--This book wants to be both evidence based and idealistic, and it fails at both. In principle, the idea is something I'm supportive of, that skills should outweigh passions in job selection. That is, that following what you're good at instead of what you love will put you in the position to have the job you want in the future. However, this is a convoluted road to follow. The author uses his own life as an overarching narrative, one in which he doesn't even follow his own four rules for evaluating one's career. I'm open to the idea that mindset is important for career success and enjoyment, and that some of these "rules" may benefit individuals, but this book is so broad yet self-contradictory that it becomes so bad that it's impossible to ignore. Thumbs down.

Title: The Bible Tells Me So: Why Defending Scripture Has Made Us Unable to Read It
Brave New Words: How AI Will Revolutionize Education (and Why That's a Good Thing)
Review: Brave New Words by Salman Khan--Khan (of KhanAcademy fame) received early access to ChatGPT 4 and is very excited about it. This book reads primarily as an advertisement for artificial intelligence in education, highlighting its potential benefits at length and glossing over any concerns. While Khan does a good job highlighting particular use cases for generative AI in education, this is a book that should have been an op-ed. It's needlessly padded given that the author refuses to engage with the risks of AI. He instead references the idea that the status quo might be worse, which even if true, doesn't abrogate a duty of care in limiting harm to the children you're educating. I feel like I'm reading sponsored content in real life. Thumbs down.

Title: The Will of the Many
Review: The Will of the Many by James Islington--Some folks had the Scholastic book fair growing up. But for my school, we could earn books by doing really well in fundraisers from Usbourne. I got some delightful books from Usbourne (which apparently is now a multi-level marketing scheme potentially), but none more treasured than books on Roman civilization. This book unlocked memories of sitting on the floor of my childhood bedroom, flipping through books with mountains of information. Here, Islington centers a decent, though bland, protagonist in a world where Roman society comes to life and also there's magic. Truly, if that doesn't hook you, I don't know what will. While the characterization is somewhat predictable, that doesn't take away from the book. Stellar pacing combined with a world that feels as though it's living and breathing around the main character makes for a delightful read, and I can't wait to see what's next. Two thumbs up.

Title: Prisoner's Dilemma: John Von Neumann, Game Theory, and the Puzzle of the Bomb
All the Worst Humans: How I Made News for Dictators, Tycoons, and Politicians
The Illusionist: The True Story of the Man Who Fooled Hitler
Amoralman: A True Story and Other Lies
Review: AMORALMAN by Derek DelGaudio--Opening with Plato and closing with Plato is a bold move for a rather short book. Yet here, it works. Perhaps predictably, this book is about the stories we tell ourselves, as well as those we let others tell us. It also touches on the nature of obsession and secrets in some unexpectedly straightforward ways. I enjoyed this book. Thumbs up.

Title: An Ocean of Minutes
Review: An Ocean of Minutes by Thea Lim--Heartfelt and complex, this short book follows a woman who gives up years of her life for her true love. It's a meditative dystopia, as she jumps years into the future to find an America/United States she doesn't recognize, and her sweetheart is nowhere to be found. There's a lot of complexity packed into the themes, as the ideas of grief and longing, of what it means to be home, and of lasting love carry across the book, but the conclusion will stick with readers for a long time, I'd imagine. Thumbs up.

Title: The Radical Fund: How a Band of Visionaries and a Million Dollars Upended America
We Had to Remove This Post
Ace of Spades
Review: Ace of Spades by Faridah Ábíké Íyímídé--This book is described as Gossip Girl meets Get Out, and it's pretty true to that. Two Black students at a prestigious private school find themselves battling against a blackmailer who seems hellbent on their social destruction. This was paced really well, with twists unfolding at almost every turn. I nearly missed a pickleball round robin for this. There is no higher praise. Thumbs up.

Title: 1929: Inside the Greatest Crash in Wall Street History–and How It Shattered a Nation
On the Edge: The Art of Risking Everything
Review: On the Edge by Nate Silver--On the plus side, you get three books for the price of one here. Silver meanders through discussions of poker and casinos/sports betting, Sam Bankman-Fried, and artificial intelligence. It does feel like he loses the plot a bit along the way, so I truthfully couldn't tell you exactly what he's going for here. His analysis of scenarios I know a decent amount about is lackluster, and his evaluation of things I'm not as knowledgeable on isn't much better in educating a layperson. All these topics are things I find interesting individually, but Silver doesn't just do a disservice to the connecting tissue of the book, he makes artificial intelligence boring. Thumbs down.

Title: A Case of Conscience
Review: A Case of Conscience by James Blish--This sci-fi book about a space traveling Jesuit priest surprisingly holds up. It was written before Vatican II, the Council convened to bring the church into the modern age, but feels fresh, exploring humanity through the lens of an outsider from an alien planet much like our own. The protagonist has a rich inner life and much of the book grapples with questions of reconciling faith and science, tradition and modernity, that still ring true today. Thumbs up.

Title: The Skeptics' Guide to the Future: What Yesterday's Science and Science Fiction Tell Us About the World of Tomorrow
Cloudmoney: Cash, Cards, Crypto and the War for our Wallets
The Handover: How We Gave Control of Our Lives to Corporations, States and AIs
Tell Me an Ending
Review: This book discusses the stories we tell ourselves and the lies we choose to live with. It does this through a sci-fi lens. The approach is, very frankly, not that elegant, but it's still a good story.

Title: Not the End of the World: How We Can Be the First Generation to Build a Sustainable Planet
