Review: Not the End of the World by Hannah Ritchie--One of the most clear books I've read, this book presents an optimistic look at the present and the future. There were times I found myself skipping ahead to the sources to review them because the data felt so counter-intuitive. For example, the number of deaths from natural disasters is dramatically decreasing over time, not increasing with climate change. With that said, this is not a climate change denial book, it is a thoughtful, in-depth examination of policy prescriptions that work and those that are just putting lipstick on a pig. Two thumbs up.

Title: Republic of Lies: American Conspiracy Theorists and Their Surprising Rise to Power
Billion Dollar Whale: The Man Who Fooled Wall Street, Hollywood, and the World
Astrophysics for People in a Hurry
Review: Astrophysics For People In A Hurry by Neil DeGrasse Tyson--Does what it says on the tin. Primer on astrophysics, where we're at and where we are going. If my review is much longer, it'll exceed the length of the book. Thumbs up.

Title: Doppelganger
Review: Doppelganger by Naomi Klein--I have a special interest in the idea of online doppelgangers. In one of the spaces I occasionally take part in online, there exists an individual who shares my first and last name. So I've experienced what Klein has, on a smaller scale, as she's often mistaken for Naomi Wolf. However, the scope of her project here is significantly broader than I thought. Rather than limiting her work to a discussion of identity, particularly as it's formed and molded (and taken over) online, she dives deep into the conspiracy theories and potent political activism which her doppelganger now engages in, as well as offering media analysis of lookalikes in fiction. It's a well-rounded book even if it's not what I was going for. Thumbs up.

Title: Supernova Era
Review: Supernova Era by Cixin Liu--Liu is at his best when he's writing about humanity's response to forces wholly outside their control. He has a good sense of instincts, primal and otherwise, which shape our collective and individual actions, and that allows him to write compelling sci-fi. Here, he imagines a supernova in relatively close proximity to us which has the unfortunate side effect of systematically killing off everyone over the age of 13. From there, perspective shifts to an almost Ender's Game format where the (possibly all precocious) kids run the show. Liu is increasingly baffling in his scope and focus as the book proceeds, but the thought experiment is a fun one which he draws to a not-so-natural conclusion. I'd say this book is fun, disturbingly delightful, and possibly the most Chinese-centric/specific of his longer books that I've read, even if America is more centered in this than any of his other works. Reading the Afterward makes clear Liu's thoughts and intents in this book, and they're touching, though perhaps more inscrutable to a Western audience. Thumbs up, even still.

Title: A History of What Comes Next
Review: This book is generally up my alley. A lot of philosophy, an intriguing sci-fi concept, some history thrown in for good measure. It all falls a little flat. The dialogue is distracting, the historical choices made are somewhat baffling, and I can't say I'm looking forward to moving on with this series.

Title: Maybe You Should Talk to Someone
Justice in Plain Sight: How a Small-Town Newspaper and Its Unlikely Lawyer Opened America's Courtrooms
The Originalism Trap: How Extremists Stole the Constitution and How We the People Can Take It Back
Review: The Originalism Trap by Madiba Dennie--This book gets at the philosophical questions present in statutory or Constitutional interpretation. Namely, one has to accept some sort of underlying interpretative philosophy in order to do any kind of consistent or rational interpretation, so why shouldn't it be originalism? Dennie refutes this assumption ardently, discussing why it doesn't work both philosophically and as applied in specific and recent cases. It's a good primer for the layperson on Supreme Court controversy in general, as well as a clear vision of a brighter future. Thumbs up.

Title: Unexpected Life: A Short History of Living Longer
Rich AF: The Winning Money Mindset That Will Change Your Life
Review: Rich AF by Vivian Tu--I've recommended I Will Teach You To Be Rich by Ramit Sethi, and I think this book will join the list of books I recommend to folks. However, that comes with a caveat, this book is only for young people. Even the young people who read it might find some of the vocab used to be cringe and lacking rizz. Tu has found incredible success, from J.P. Morgan to Buzzfeed to TikTok financial guru, and shares it all here. I'm always impressed when I learn anything new in personal finance, and this book had that in spades, all in accessible language that was fully comprehensible. Thumbs up.

Title: Stories of Your Life and Others
Moneyland: Why Thieves and Crooks Now Rule the World and How To Take It Back
Review: Moneyland by Oliver Bullough--This book deals with the ins and outs of money laundering. From its mechanisms to its victims, Bullough exposes the world as it is: thousands of international companies assigned to individual addresses, trusts which systematically evade laws against perpetuities, and common people, in rich companies and poor, who suffer as a result. He tracks kleptocracy and tax evasion from tiny islands to the Ukraine, from London to Nevada. It's a swirling tempest of money and power, infuriating yet perhaps unavoidable. Thumbs up.

Title: Slow Productivity: The Lost Art of Accomplishment Without Burnout
Undivided: The Quest for Racial Solidarity in an American Church
Review: Undivided by Hahrie Han--I am pretty torn with how I feel about this. The book wants to be inspiring, relating anecdotes about an anti racist church program. But it's largely unsuccessful. The author proposes that a state that voted for universal preschool but also voted for Trump is a contradiction that can only be explained by a megachurch's antiracism program. This proposition is never fully explored, and indeed, the intriguing alternate question exists--if an antiracism program was so successful as to change voting patterns where people supported universal preschool, why didn't it change them to vote against Trump? Since I think fundamentally there's issues with the author's assumptions here, I can't respect the book as much. Thumbs down.

Title: The Family Next Door: The Heartbreaking Imprisonment of the Thirteen Turpin Siblings and Their Extraordinary Rescue
Operation Paperclip: The Secret Intelligence Program that Brought Nazi Scientists to America
The Merge
Review: The Merge by Grace Walker--Some books pride themselves on being body horror, this book is something else entirely, mind horror. It focuses on a woman contemplating Merging with her mother in a desperate bid to cure her mother's Alzheimer's disease. Both women keep secrets from the other that define the plot, and the story turns out to be poignant and terrifying. Thumbs up.

Title: Without You, There Is No Us: My Time with the Sons of North Korea's Elite
An Unkindness of Ghosts
Review: An Unkindness of Ghosts by River Solomon--There are many things this book does well. In depicting a future where humanity largely exists on a single ship, Solomon does a phenomenal job of fleshing out the world and making it lived-in and realistic. They do this through the lens of a relatable protagonist and stakes that feel dire and really drive the story forward. This is a really solid debut novel. Thumbs up.

Title: What If?: Serious Scientific Answers to Absurd Hypothetical Questions
Proving Ground: The Untold Story of the Six Women Who Programmed the World's First Modern Computer
Proud Boys and the White Ethnostate: How the Alt-Right Is Warping the American Imagination
Never Saw Me Coming: How I Outsmarted the FBI and the Entire Banking System—and Pocketed $40 Million
Review: Never Saw Me Coming by Tanya Smith--Gripping and surprisingly emotional, this memoir follows a woman who scammed banks in the United States out of millions of dollars. She describes unbelievable heists and her adventures laundering that money, which as you know, is not a victimless crime. Smith is not a sympathetic figure yet tries to evoke sympathy, which is, erm, a bit annoying. I'm certainly open to the idea that disparities in race leads to bad decisions in our justice system. I'm less sympathetic to the idea that a woman who has escaped multiple times from federal prison somehow deserves much leniency. Despite this, it's a good book. Thumbs up.

Title: Who Could That Be at This Hour?
Liars & Outliers: Enabling the Trust That Society Needs to Thrive
Review: Liars and Outliers by Bruce Schneier--I read this on the recommendation of one of my work class instructors today. We've been spending a lot of time in this course talking about information security, so I expected a dry look at keeping digital assets secure, but I was surprised at what I found. Schneier here looks at incentive structures, using philosophy, psychology, and game theory to demonstrate that much of the malicious activity in the world is rational (to one extent or another), and proposes paths forward. He takes simple aphorisms like "You are what you reward" and fleshes them out, explaining how it applies to tax avoidance, the existence and negligence of multi-national corporations, and driving. It's a fun, rich book. Thumbs up.

Title: The Devil at His Elbow: Alex Murdaugh and the Fall of a Southern Dynasty
Without Precedent: How Chief Justice Roberts and His Accomplices Rewrote the Constitution and Dismantled Our Rights
Laboratories of Autocracy: A Wake-Up Call from Behind the Lines
The Vegetarian
Review: The Vegetarian by Han Kang--Disturbing at times, this book revolves around power and agency taken to logical conclusions. It starts with a simple decision, a woman decides not to eat meat. (Technically I think she embraces veganism, but I don't judge) Her decision ends up impacting herself, her relationships, and power dynamics in the community structures she's involved with, and the story is artfully told in three different parts from three perspectives, but never her own. It's a chilling, carnal story that is methodical in its approach. Thumbs up.

Title: Conspiracy: Why the Rational Believe the Irrational
Close Your Eyes and Count to 10
Pathfinders: Extraordinary Stories of People Like You on the Quest for Financial Independence—And How to Join Them
Review: Pathfinders by JL Collins--I wasn't expecting to enjoy this book as much as I did. It's a no-frills collection of anecdotes from folks that have followed Collins' Simple Path to Wealth. There's plenty of success stories in here, but what I really appreciated is that it's not just folks that have followed his plan to a t. Instead, there's a diversity of thought and experiences, folks who retired early and others who plan to work until they're 70+. What unites them all is the idea that financial independence doesn't have to be a distant goal and it doesn't have to be complex. A final great feature is the author's interstitials with common sense advice. Thumbs up.

Title: The Monk of Mokha
Review: The Monk of Mokha by Dave Eggers-- I've loved Eggers' other work, particularly The Circle, but this is a different direction entirely. The nonfiction work focuses on a man's journey, both literal and metaphorical, to bring Yemeni coffee to the world. The real stories here are snappy and action-packed, but also connect to some idea of the American dream. I really enjoyed this one. Two thumbs up.

Title: It's All a Game: The History of Board Games from Monopoly to Settlers of Catan
The Babysitter: My Summers with a Serial Killer
Strength in Numbers: How Polls Work and Why We Need Them
The Knowledge Machine: How Irrationality Created Modern Science
youthjuice
Review: youthjuice by E.K. Sathue--Meh. I'm not really captured by this book. It's up my alley, a satire of the beauty and influencer industries, a large business hiding a dark secret. But I almost quit reading this because I just didn't care enough about what was going on. I don't think the characterization is bad, per se, but the main character's vapid thought processes are almost too lackluster because there's nothing going on there. There's nothing to invest in. Other than youthjuice. Thumbs down.

Title: Dying for a Paycheck: How Modern Management Harms Employee Health and Company Performance--And What We Can Do about It
Singularity Sky
Review: Singularity Sky by Charles Stross--I didn't really understand this book until I looked up its Wikipedia page after. I think I needed the context of this being more than 20 years old. It makes way more sense, then, as a political satire of the Soviet Union and the "free" West than a modern day analysis. With that in mind, the book is good and even thought-provoking, but is weakest when it's explicitly political. Dialogue parroting political debates from the 90s compares poorly to the implicit politicization of the different factions. In short, the book is best when it shows, not tells, its world building. Thumbs up.

Title: The Wolves of K Street: The Secret History of How Big Money Took Over Big Government
Rejection
Contributors: Thijs van Nimwegen (Translator), Arjaan van Nimwegen (Translator)
Review: Rejection by Tony Tulathimutte--I don't think I've read a book more plugged into the Discourse. Framed as a series of interconnected short stories, the central theme, as the title indicates, is rejection. Social rejection, romantic rejection, online rejection, it's all covered in truly pain-inducing detail. These are, by and large, not sympathetic protagonists. And yet, with that in mind, this is the first time I've read a book and audibly groaned in empathy. From the "absurdly woke" to the sexually deviant to the meta-editorial critique of an author who has taken too many liberties, there's not a single story here that I wasn't amazed by. I am both captivated and living in dread. Two thumbs up.

Title: The Hike
Review: The Hike by Drew Magary--This book is a great, easy plane read. Think an adult Alice in Wonderland in a world where Alice in Wonderland already exists as a fictional work. Thus, a character who is self aware of tropes, how ridiculous this whole thing is, etc. The book is fantastical, leaping in turns from wry wit to profound emotional resonance. Delightful. Thumbs up.

Title: The Pentagon's Brain: An Uncensored History of DARPA, America's Top-Secret Military Research Agency
Playing God: American Catholic Bishops and The Far Right
Reagan: His Life and Legend
Review: Reagan by Max Boot--Biographies are hard. As I was reading this book today, I imagined the difficulty in writing a biography of someone I know well. How do you take their decisions in adulthood and contextualize them using their upbringing or other defining moments. Now, that problem reaches incredible proportions when applied to a well-known subject that the biographer likely did not know well. This is a dense book, I think it's the longest I will have read this year. But every page is needed to dive into the life of Reagan, and more material still gets left, by necessity, to the imagination. Boot paints a picture of a complicated man, particularly from a policy perspective. On almost any position the President is known for, from labor to fiscal conservatism to gun control to abortion, his policy and personal records are far from straightforward. Concurrently, his personal life is complicated and wrought with tension and tragedy throughout. Thumbs up.

Title: The Worst Trickster Story Ever Told: Native America, the Supreme Court, and the U. S. Constitution
Review: The Worst Trickster Story Ever Told by Keith Richotte Jr.--It's not often that I get to read books recommended by Neil Gorsuch. In reading some of his opinions early this year, however, I found just that. This book seeks to find the basis for federal plenary power over otherwise sovereign Indian nations. The result here is a comprehensive overview of the ways in which the Court continues to whitewash its own precedent and the law itself. I appreciate the thoughtful resolution proposed to the current quagmire of laws and court cases that enmesh the problem of Indian sovereignty. But ultimately his approach is idealistic and fails to consider even basic rejoinders that imperils its implementation. It's still a well-written and witty book. Thumbs up.

Title: Aberration in the Heartland of the Real: The Secret Lives of Timothy McVeigh
My Lovely Wife
Review: My Lovely Wife by Samantha Downing--Dripping with tension, this book is about a husband and wife stuck in a cycle they seemingly can't escape. Many couples deal with this sort of thing. Addiction, violence, arguments, and so on. The unforchy thing is that this couple is caught in a cycle of murder. It's a riveting story and deals with some ideas, like the difficulties of parenting in the modern day, really well. Thumbs up.

Title: On the Clock: What Low-Wage Work Did to Me and How It Drives America Insane
Pegasus: How a Spy in Your Pocket Threatens the End of Privacy, Dignity, and Democracy
Life After Power: Seven Presidents and Their Search for Purpose Beyond the White House
Review: Life After Power by Jared Cohen--This is a book about one of the most bewildering positions one could occupy: former leader of the free world. By visiting the post-presidency lives of Jefferson, Quincy Adams, Cleveland, Taft, Hoover, Carter, and W. Bush, Cohen is able to reframe the office of the President itself. I usually think of these types of profiles as kind of gimmicky and lackluster, if interesting. But this book in particular is gripping. It brings just enough context from the presidency itself to inform the analysis of post-presidency life. Truly a delight. Two thumbs up.

Title: Pirate Enlightenment, or the Real Libertalia
Horror Movie
Review: Horror Movie by Paul Tremblay--Reading horror when I was young really shaped my experience with literature and reading generally. Sure, other genres spark curiosity or engagement, but fear? It's a unique feeling for your brain to basically turn words on a page into pictures that can fundamentally shake you. This book recaptured that feeling for me. Alternating from screenplay to a traditional book, this fictional recounting of the creation of a never-released horror movie is bone-chilling. One scene after another, the story unfolds, and the dread incurred along the way leaps off the page. Thumbs up.

Title: The Midnight Feast
Review: The Midnight Feast by Lucy Foley--This book is if White Lotus met Midsommar, had a quick one-night tryst, and ended up abandoning the accidental baby with Dr. Phil. The story is fine, but honestly a bit exhausting. The shifting perspectives are not differentiated well and the reveals are, at best, frustrating. Not to put too fine a point on it, but the titular feast is itself disappointing and poorly resolved. Thumbs down.

Title: Shelterwood
Review: Shelterwood by Lisa Wingate--Powerful and vibrant, this book focuses on two women nearly a century apart in rural Oklahoma. The parallels in these stories are not all that subtle, but they work well. Wingate did her research and her experience in Oklahoma shines through. She references people and places in this state that someone with a surface level knowledge wouldn't get while crafting a really loving and hopeful story. Similar to a book like This Tender Land, this story references some clear atrocities that took place in Oklahoma, demonstrating how the ramifications pass from generation to generation. There are still people alive today who were directly impacted by gross mistreatment of Indians here in Indian territory after it was subsumed into the state. This book is a solid chronicle of our history while telling a darn good story. Thumbs up.

Title: The Book of Doors
Review: The Book of Doors by Gareth Brown--Look, I love a good time travel story as much as anyone. But this book, playing with time as much as it is the reader's heartstrings, is very well done. It opens with a woman working at a bookstore and finding a book that allows her to open doors anywhere in the world. From there, the story remains surprisingly grounded, operating with a small cast of characters and a lot of emotional resonance. I really enjoyed this. Two thumbs up.

Title: The Myth of Sisyphus and Other Essays
The Perfect Predator: A Scientist's Race to Save Her Husband from a Deadly Superbug: A Memoir
The Husbands
Review: The Husbands by Holly Gramazio--I've been thinking a lot lately about the lines separating desire from contentment. While the two aren't mutually exclusive, certainly, creating a balance can be difficult. This book ultimately is about choice. When a woman finds a husband she did not ask for or want in her apartment, she is concerned. When this husband, and every subsequent husband, disappears to be replaced by another when they go into the apartment attic, she is dismayed. Much like Matt Haig's Midnight Library, this is a story of choosing between the best possible version of your life and feeling as though you're settling. It's short and fun. Thumbs up.

Title: Autocracy Inc: The Dictators Who Want to Run the World
Review: Autocracy, Inc. by Anne Applebaum--I've read some good books on kleptocratic structures in the past, but this one does a really good job of highlighting the link and even dependency between authoritarian/autocratic regimes and robust international networks. This is a short primer on the subject, while also serving as an urgent call to action for democratic countries. I think some of the key points about autocracy begetting and reinforcing autocracy are particularly unsettling. Thumbs up.

Title: The Bill of Obligations: The Ten Habits of Good Citizens
Incidents Around the House
Review: Incidents Around the House by Josh Malerman--In short, this is the most scary horror book I've ever read. The whole book is from the perspective of a child who is seeing strange things around her home, and as the story escalates and the stakes rise, that narrative framing is chillingly effective. I won't forget the experience of reading this book for a long time. Two thumbs up.

Title: The Skripal Files: The Life and Near Death of a Russian Spy
Firefighting: The Financial Crisis and Its Lessons
Authors: Henry M. Paulson, Timothy F. Geithner, Ben S. Bernanke

Title: A Wolf at the Schoolhouse Door: The Dismantling of Public Education and the Future of School
Review: A Wolf At The Schoolhouse Door by Jack Schneider and Jennifer Berkshire--This book is somehow both tensely horrifying and barely scratches the surface of the battle for education in America. The authors detail a history of education privatization, beginning largely with Reagan and coming into full fruition in the last few years. I think there are some legitimate concerns with some public schools which the authors gloss over, but they do a convincing job of demonstrating that introducing a profit motive into the educational process is rarely workable or beneficial for kids. Thumbs up.

Title: I Know What I Saw: Modern-Day Encounters with Monsters of New Urban Legend and Ancient Lore
The Breakdown
Review: The Breakdown by B. A. Paris--A psychological thriller that somehow exceeds expectations, this book follows a schoolteacher who encounters a woman late at night on a rural road, finding her own life forever changed. The book is impeccably paced, featuring an unreliable narrator who grows increasingly paranoid that she is being watched. I loved how much care was taken in this book, and its twists pay off incredibly well. Two thumbs up.

Title: Quanta and Fields: The Biggest Ideas in the Universe
Conclave
Review: Conclave by Robert Harris--Certainly more of a political thriller than a religious treatise, this book dives deep into a fictional conclave to decide a new Pope, and the vote whipping and dealing which potentially undergirds it. The tension between doing what is practical and what is right in the eyes of God really sets the tone, and for a story about a bunch of cardinals getting together and following administrative procedure, it's pretty solid. Thumbs up.

Title: Private Finance, Public Power: A History of Bank Supervision in America
Nuclear War : A Scenario
Review: Nuclear War by Annie Jacobsen--I truly don't know if there could be a more sobering read. This book imagines, in painstaking detail, a hypothetical nuclear scenario if North Korea launched strikes at the United States. Second by second and minute by minute, time ticks by. The exhaustive research Jacobsen conducted really comes out here. Interviews with a plethora of current and former military and FEMA leaders and strategists come together to create this hyper-realistic scenario. Thumbs up.

Title: I Hope This Finds You Well
Review: I Hope This Finds You Well by Natalie Sue--Slow burn romance is generally not my thing. But this book contains multitudes. It begins with a woman lashing out against her colleagues and dealing with the machinations of a soulless HR department. It ends with some truly funny moments, satire of bullshit jobs everywhere, and a keen look at some serious ideas. I don't love or even like the protagonist, but the book does a great job of contemplating loneliness and depression in a relatable way. Thumbs up.

Title: The Measure
Review: The Measure by Nikki Erlick--There's a lot of dread and hope in this book. In that way, it subverts the "feel good" genre that invites folks to consider things like legacy and the impact one life can have. This book opens with every adult on earth receiving a simple string which predicts how long they have left to live. Dread comes in from the examination of groups and our tendencies to construct narratives around and wield power against the "other". But there's also a lot of hope here in the meditation on what love means and the beauty in irrationality. It's a smooth read. Thumbs up.

Title: The Ballad of Songbirds and Snakes
Truth in Our Times: Inside the Fight for Press Freedom in the Age of Alternative Facts
The Instability of Truth: Brainwashing, Mind Control, and Hyper-Persuasion
Stolen Pride: Loss, Shame, and the Rise of the Right
Review: Stolen Pride by Arlie Russell Hothschild--Theories on the support for Donald Trump and the revitalization of white supremacy are a dime a dozen. This yearslong analysis of a congressional district in Kentucky, however, is a little different. It approaches the economic and social realities for folks in Appalachia from a dual-sided perspective of pride and shame. I'm not sure how much I buy into the conclusions, but I am sure that the stories and people here are worth listening to. Thumbs up.

Title: Private Revolutions: Coming of Age in a New China
Review: Private Revolutions by Yuan Yang--Filled with optimism and pain, this book explores the lives of four women in modern China. Profiling an entrepreneur, a labor activist, a factory worker, and a professional, Yang details the substantial roadblocks facing women in China today. The book is insightful, drawing from years of on the ground experience. Thumbs up.

Title: Truth Bombs: Confronting the Lies Conservatives Believe (To Our Own Demise)
If Anyone Builds It, Everyone Dies: Why Superhuman AI Would Kill Us All
Changing Our Mind: Definitive 3rd Edition of the Landmark Call for Inclusion of LGBTQ Christians with Response to Critics
The Digital Silk Road: China's Quest to Wire the World and Win the Future
The Hole
Review: The Hole by Hye-Young Pyun--Slow and intimate, the story here focuses on a man who is recovering from a coma following a car accident which kills his wife. As Oghi struggles to regain his physical capabilities, he has ample time to consider his life amid periods of isolation and neglect. There's a not-so-subtle metaphor throughout of the hole Oghi's mother-in-law digs for a new koi pond. The book is both dark and poignant. Thumbs up.

Title: Factfulness: Ten Reasons We're Wrong About the World – and Why Things Are Better Than You Think
Around the World in Eighty Games: From Tarot to Tic-Tac-Toe, Catan to Chutes and Ladders, a Mathematician Unlocks the Secrets of the World's Greatest Games
Review: Around the World in Eighty Games by Marcus du Sautoy--Delightful and educational, this book explores the history of games, both classic and modern, from across the globe. This concept would be fun in and of itself, but the author is a mathematician whose love of the underlying numbers in these games is incredibly charming. You'll forgive him the sin of some of his eighty "games" not actually being games because the journey is worthwhile. Thumbs up.

Title: Sherlock Holmes and the Sussex Sea-Devils
Word Nerd: Dispatches from the Games, Grammar, and Geek Underground
Critical Mass
Review: Critical Mass by Daniel Suarez--Suarez has written some of my favorite books about the future in Daemon and Freedom (TM). This marks the first time in decades that he has returned to the well and written a follow-up book (to the space mining book Delta-V). Call it a future for a new generation, perhaps. Suarez lays out a new visionary look focusing on space ethics and mining, though his idea of a decentralized space society may be even more fantastical than the self-sustaining cities of Freedom (TM). Regardless, it's an enthralling ride. Thumbs up.

Title: Confident Women: Swindlers, Grifters, and Shapeshifters of the Feminine Persuasion
The Year of Living Constitutionally: One Man's Humble Quest to Follow the Constitution's Original Meaning
Review: A Year of Living Constitutionally by A.J. Jacobs--This new book from Jacobs treats the Constitution with wit and careful respect, as he attempts to document a year of living according to the Constitution's precepts. Along the way, he paints a fair/nuanced view of originalism vs. living constitutionalism while finding a newfound love for the country. In this attempt to "play fair", I think he gives unearned credence to some particular judicial philosophies, but that's just my bias creeping in. Thumbs up.

Title: Prophet
Review: Prophet by Helen Macdonald and Sin Blaché--Science fiction (or maybe speculative fiction? I get confused) at its best, this book follows a team exploring a mysterious substance that provokes extreme nostalgia. While the plot itself is somewhat meandering, almost dreamlike, it's also one of the most impactful books I've read this year. Characterization is excellent while the story is thought-provoking. Two thumbs up.

Title: The Men Who Stare at Goats
The New New Thing: A Silicon Valley Story
How Should We Then Live? The Rise and Decline of Western Thought and Culture
Over Ruled: The Human Toll of Too Much Law
Review: Over Ruled by Neil Gorsuch--This is an interesting book. Gorsuch identifies some good anecdotes detailing instances where federal law or agency activity, in his view, overreaches. I'd certainly hesitate to suggest I think a Supreme Court justice is wrong about the law, but perhaps I could share some critiques of the arguments he chose to use. For example, he has a central thesis on federal overreach, but gets in detail on state law/agencies that also overreach. This isn't a problem in and of itself, but it is somewhat baffling, particularly with the amount of energy the Justice spends talking about the Founders and the role of Congress. It's in that lengthy exploration, too, that facts appear to be misleading. Gorsuch accurately describes the "state of play" at the time of the Founders, but conveniently leaves out historical context+-the very decisions railed against by some Founders only have these notable dissents recorded because...as a whole, the early Congress DID outsource functions to the executive branch. Whether you agree with that as a policy decision or not, it's tough to be an originalist without considering that this structure is baked into the fabric of this country. Finally, I'm intrigued by the Justice's statements on agency rule-making (a public and transparent process). He mentions that the number of rules has gone up over time, which as a general rule, is true! But he presents this as itself a bad thing, as well as the fact that few people comment on the rule proposals. The reality is that most rules slightly modify/improve other rules and have no bearing on the vast majority of citizens, the lack of input is a feature, not a bug. The Justice who helped in overturning Chevron should appreciate the fact that regulated industries/citizens don't tend to get regulated in mass quantities, with broad sweeping individual rules, but incrementally and methodically. Perhaps it's fair to say that this is just as the Founders intended. Sideways thumb.

Title: The Hidden Globe: How Wealth Hacks the World
Review: The Hidden Globe by Atossa Abrahamian--I often say, tongue-in-cheek, that money laundering is not a victimless crime. This book dives into that reality, exploring the "placeless places" that both the richest and most marginalized folks in the world populate. It's a fascinating exploration of the offshore detention centers, outer space exploration, corporation-controlled charter cities, and free trade zones that those who don't have or don't want association with a nation-state can pursue. Thumbs up.

Title: The Future of Geography: How Power and Politics in Space will Change Our World
Review: The Future of Geography by Tim Marshall--This is a fun ride. It's an exploration of our journey to the Moon and beyond, with an emphasis on modern day geopolitical tensions and legal loopholes that stand in the way of interplanetary exploration and commerce. At the same time, though, the author is very suspicious of China's intentions in space, making that very clear, while being surprisingly forgiving of the United States' efforts there. That's fine, but it kind of undermines his late-book vision of a world where international peace is acquired because of space exploration somehow. Despite this discrepancy, the book is fun and educational. Thumbs up.

Title: Empire of AI: Dreams and Nightmares in Sam Altman's OpenAI
Review: Empire of AI by Karen Hao--This book explores the origins and existence of OpenAI, charting a clear path from rise to crises. Hao does a good job of keeping a sharp critique of Altman and OpenAI front and center while ensuring this critique is also broadly applicable. For example, the detailed analysis of how AI currently exploits labor and land is probably the best part of the book. Accordingly, it's little surprise that the worst part of the book is more personal critique of Altman himself. Hao's reporting gives great insight into the backroom dealings around Altman's firing and quick reinstatement by the board of OpenAI, as well as OpenAI's transition to a for-profit (public benefit) company. However, Hao's detailing of particularly sensitive allegations against Altman uses extremely uncharitable language that appears to merely transition the reader from one crisis to another. The allegations are left as an afterthought, with an individual specifically biased against Altman getting the final word on their veracity. The book is good, but that poor choice is hard to stomach. With that said, this is a good, important book. Thumbs up.

Title: Evicted: Poverty and Profit in the American City
Review: Evicted by Matthew Desmond--This is a tough read. Desmond installed himself in Milwaukee in 2008 and 2009 observing and documenting a housing crisis in progress, a familiar story in many American cities today. In the process, he identifies multiple policy issues with the broader American approach to low-income housing, assistance programs, and the eviction process. The people involved, landlords and tenants alike, are flawed and charming, with their own hopes and foibles chronicled here. Desmond takes a hard look, between anecdotes and data, at housing in America, and the reader may not like what's reflected back. Thumbs up.

Title: American Sherlock: Murder, Forensics, and the Birth of American Csi
The Ministry of Time
Review: The Ministry of Time by Kaliane Bradley--What a fun book. It's part sci-fi time travel book, part romantic comedy, part workplace sitcom, and all heart. The story draws you in from the start, and I really enjoyed the places it went. Thanks, Obama. Thumbs up.

Title: The Ultimate Hidden Truth of the World…: Essays
Decisions and Dissents of Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg: A Selection
The Food Lab: Better Home Cooking Through Science
Justice on the Brink: The Death of Ruth Bader Ginsburg, the Rise of Amy Coney Barrett, and Twelve Months That Transformed the Supreme Court
The Bee Sting
Review: The Bee Sting by Paul Murray--Engrossing and impactful, this book follows a family in the aftermath of the 2008 financial crisis. Every family member is living a life of secrets, and the story is so excellently told. In some ways, it's a meditation on the nature of life in community, but it's also a slow burn of a thriller that has maybe one of the most devastating endings I've read. It's wholly worthwhile. Thumbs up.

Title: Rogues: True Stories of Grifters, Killers, Rebels and Crooks
Area 51: An Uncensored History of America's Top Secret Military Base
Review: Area 51 by Annie Jacobsen--In Nuclear War, Jacobsen made the unimaginable relatable, and a new nuclear war read as fresh as tomorrow's newspaper. Here, she makes the most fabled military and intelligence area on the planet nearly open and inviting. Superb writing details the origins of spy planes, nuclear troubles, and alien conspiracies. Some will call her just another crony of the deep state, but she established credibility convincingly. The final chapter and epilogue are among the most impactful things I've read. Two thumbs up.

Title: The Manor House
Review: The Manor House by Gilly Macmillan--The concept for this book is simple. A couple wins the lottery, builds their dream home, and later, the wife finds her husband dead. The treat of this book is the writing from multiple perspectives, including the main suspects. The plot itself is convoluted in an enjoyable way. This was a decent source of peace and fun in a chaotic day. Thumbs up.

Title: A City on Mars: Can We Settle Space, Should We Settle Space, and Have We Really Thought This Through?
If This Book Exists, You're in the Wrong Universe
The Prosecutor: One Man's Battle to Bring Nazis to Justice
The Last Greatest Magician in the World: Howard Thurston versus Houdini & the Battles of the American Wizards
The Singularity is Nearer
Review: The Singularity Is Nearer by Ray Kurzweil--How often do you get to be vindicated on twenty year old predictions? In this book, Kurzweil argues that he has been proven right and that folks should fully buy into his belief that the Singularity, such as it is, is just around the corner. One of my favorite sections of the book deals with both the role of progress over the last two centuries in health and wealth, as well as getting to the reasons why we don't accept good news at face value. I'm not a true believer, but Kurzweil's optimism is contagious, and his belief in what's coming down the pike over the next 20 years is fascinating, if nothing else. Thumbs up.

Title: Dark Persuasion: A History of Brainwashing from Pavlov to Social Media
Now What?: How to Move Forward When We're Divided
Red Lines: Political Cartoons and the Struggle Against Censorship
The Grandest Game
Review: The Grandest Game by Jennifer Lynn Barnes--I had rude words to say about the last book in this series, and how that book seemed like it was written exclusively to set up this series. That is still true. This book does a good reset of some firmly established characters and dynamics while driving the series forward. Is it good in its own right? I'm still somewhat disappointed with this book not telling one self-contained story. It's very [Dystopian YA Film] Part One, which isn't a good look. But I will tamp down my criticism, because the joy of being back and unpacking new intrigue is very fun. Thumbs up.

Title: You Weren't Meant to Be Human
Darrow's Nightmare: The Forgotten Story of America's Most Famous Trial Lawyer
Bloodline
Review: It's no secret that I love Star Wars politics, and this is no exception. It's post-Disney acquisition, so Luke is out of the picture on his remote planet, and Han largely is too. Leia, as a Senator in the New Republic, is trying to keep the Republic from falling apart, and is on the struggle bus. Good time, and honestly really thoughtful not just about what it means to rebel, but what the ramifications are when the revolution happens.

Title: A Half-Built Garden
Review: A Half-Built Garden by Ruthanna Emrys--Vibrantly optimistic, this book opens with a dilemma. We make first contact with an advanced alien species, they're willing to share all their advancements, under the condition that we begin moving to leave Earth, a planet we've corrupted by our own actions. Set 60 years in the future, the book depicts a society that's almost wholly foreign to today--and there's also aliens. The story is thought-provoking, the world is thought-through, and the science is thought-leading. Thumbs up.

Title: Superminds: The Surprising Power of People and Computers Thinking Together
The Constitution in Jeopardy: An Unprecedented Effort to Rewrite Our Fundamental Law and What We Can Do About It
The Ferryman
Review: The Ferryman by Justin Cronin--Unsettling yet beautiful, this book follows life on an island that's a little too perfect. Every need is provided for, life continues until the point that folks need to be reborn, and the process begins again. I enjoyed this book, but I thought the plot twist was pretty underwhelming. The book's story leaves something to be desired, but the journey is still intriguing, and there are still folks I'd recommend it to. Thumbs up.

Title: Chaos: Charles Manson, the CIA, and the Secret History of the Sixties
Review: Chaos by Tom O'Neill--What do you get when you combine MK-Ultra, the assassination of JFK, and an in-depth look at the Manson murders? Frustratingly for the author, he doesn't know. So the story, then, becomes the search for the story itself. This started as a passion project for a journalist in the 1990s who thought elements of the book Helter Skelter, by the prosecutor against Manson, sounded fishy. It ended decades later with an author who traversed the country multiple times, interviewed hundreds of people, and despite significant new information which differs substantially from the trial that ended in death sentences for the Manson family, still can't draw a satisfying conclusion. It's a fun, thrilling read, particularly for those with an interest in the journalistic process. Thumbs up.

Title: We Came to Welcome You
Review: We Came To Welcome You by Vincent Tirado--A spooky book that never quite leans fully into its unsettling nature, the story follows a queer couple that moves into an exciting new suburban neighborhood! Terror ensues where terror ordinarily ensues, the Homeowner's Association. There's a weird thread in the narrative related to reclaiming power or something that honestly just doesn't hit that well for me, but in general, I enjoyed this book. Thumbs up.

Title: Brotopia: Breaking Up the Boys' Club of Silicon Valley
Review: Brotopia by Emily Chang--An incisive history of "bro culture" in Silicon Valley, this book explores the myth of male dominance at early tech companies like Google. It's fairly comprehensive in examining outcomes for women and minorities in STEM, while taking time to highlight the companies that have made demonstrable steps toward achieving equity, as they define it. Reading this book with the context of currently working for a company mired in a series of scandals in sexual harassment was also edifying: the large tech companies have uniformly taken steps to protect against harassment that my agency hasn't opted for. This book really does hit every element, from hiring to working as an employee to promotions, venture capital, having a family and working, and even being a female/minority end user of these tech products all receive distinct focus. It's a good summary. Thumbs up.

Title: Hidden Pictures
Review: Hidden Pictures by Jason Rekulak--Wow. I wasn't expecting a whole lot, but this book surprised me. It has a truly unique premise for a psychological thriller and keeps up a pace that doesn't stop. The story deals really well with addiction and a variety of sensitive subjects while propelling forward a plot that, for a variety of reasons, I've never seen before. Truly well done. Two thumbs up.

Title: The Witching Year: A Memoir of Earnest Fumbling Through Modern Witchcraft
Review: The Witching Year by Diana Helmuth-- sometimes it is hard to keep up with all the religious "years" books. After A.J. Jacobs spent a year living biblically, sacrificially devoting your life to a new religious tradition and writing about it feels like an easy cash grab. This take on it is interesting largely because the compromises the author makes really reflect society. So, for example, Helmuth acknowledges that the Wiccan/Witchcraft tradition is basically exclusively appropriation. But her answer to that isn't to back away or even to acknowledge the roots of these traditions, it's to eventually ignore that uncomfortable fact. Sadly, I think the book is harmed by Helmuth not going all-in-one on some of her findings, and the moral of the story, that Witchcraft is about finding one's own desires, lacks punch or grit. Sideways thumb.

Title: The Hate Next Door: Undercover within the New Face of White Supremacy
Review: The Hate Next Door by Matson and Tawni Browning--Here, Browning focuses on the white supremacy that lies, not dormant, but present, in unsuspecting American communities. They bring their collective experience going undercover in white supremacist community to bear in this book chock-full of anecdotes that would seem impossible if they didn't come from highly credible sources. From everyday Americans to churches to houses of faith, Browning draws a frightening picture of organizations, some firm and structured, others loose and decentralized, operating across the United States. Thumbs up.

Title: All That We See or Seem
Review: An exciting speculative thriller set in the near future, this book features many themes that hit close to home, particularly considering the potential impact of AI in the near term.

Title: Food for Thought: Essays and Ruminations
Get Rich or Lie Trying: Ambition and Deceit in the New Influencer Economy
Review: Get Rich Or Lie Trying by Symeon Brown--This book is fairly surface level in getting into influencer culture and the associated scams that go with it. From work-from-home fake jobs to rugpull crypto "assets" to dropshipping collectives, all are mentioned. I think I'd have liked a little more substance, but it's a perfectly fine primer. Sideways thumb.

Title: Ticking Clock: Behind the Scenes at 60 Minutes
Confirmation Bias: Inside Washington's War Over the Supreme Court, from Scalia's Death to Justice Kavanaugh
Freezing Order: A True Story of Money Laundering, Murder, and Surviving Vladimir Putin's Wrath
The Art of Gathering: How We Meet and Why It Matters
Review: The Art of Gathering by Priya Parker--I made the mistake of thinking that I am the target audience for this book. I am not. The book is about intentional gatherings of all types and the art of hosting them. However, it's much more written for the conference and corporate off-site host than the board game night host. Considerations of space, openings and closing, and generous authority are fun, but perhaps literally above my pay grade. Despite this, I think there's wisdom to be gained for anyone who might read it. Thumbs up.

Title: Everybody Lies: Big Data, New Data, and What the Internet Can Tell Us about Who We Really Are
Review: Everybody Lies by Seth Stephen-Davidowitz--Adding humanity to data feels intuitively impossible, but it's the primary goal of this book. The author recounts various stories of data, usually polling data, coupled with Google search history, explaining both fascinating findings and the perils of using Big Data to draw conclusions. From crime and violent movies to race and political preferences, it's educational and relatable every step of the way. Thumbs up.

Title: The Angel Makers: Arsenic, a Midwife, and Modern History's Most Astonishing Murder Ring
Review: The Angel Makers by Patti McCracken--Unsettling. This book recounts a perhaps little known true crime story. In 1920s Hungary, a woman, the village midwife, kills, and kills again, and convinces other women to kill their husbands, and collect inheritances, and so on. The account is exhaustively researched and the import of the story is never lost on the author. It's a troubling tale with surprising present day ramifications. Thumbs up.

Title: 2030: How Today's Biggest Trends Will Collide and Reshape the Future of Everything
The Oldest Book in the World: Philosophy in the Age of the Pyramids
Billionaires' Row: Tycoons, High Rollers, and the Epic Race to Build the World's Most Exclusive Skyscrapers
Beyond Measure: The Hidden History of Measurement from Cubits to Quantum Constants
Imminent: Inside the Pentagon's Hunt for UFOs
Review: Imminent by Luis Elisando--This theoretically nonfiction account examines the life of a former senior intelligence officer in his own words. I have no quarrels with the way it's written, per se, but as with most alien/UAP disclosure accounts, I ultimately have to question why. That is, if it's true that most major corporations and government agencies at the highest possible levels have been complicit in systematically concealing extraterrestrial activity for decades, what has changed to allow a book like this to be published? Without a satisfying answer on that front, it's tough to think of this as anything other than a fanciful elaboration of reality. Sideways thumb.

Title: Enlightenment Now: The Case for Reason, Science, Humanism, and Progress
Review: Enlightenment Now by Steven Pinker--It's an easy week for some to allege that the world sucks. I have a feeling that the author of this book would still suggest that things are quite all right. Who's right? Who's to say? What I can tell you is that this book is optimistic yet clear-headed, approaching with data the idea that humanity is progressing, sometimes in spite of those who claim to desire progress. It's both thoughtful and thought-provoking. While Pinker doesn't explicitly ask the reader this, I found myself thinking something I've been considering quite a bit lately: What would it mean to believe that the world is good and getting better? How would it color the world and society? I'm not talking about a blind or naive optimism that ignores the issues that exist, but the idea of putting aside "existential" threats and focusing on history as precedent for a brighter future is certainly appealing. And if nothing else, in this book, it's well-argued. Two thumbs up.

Title: Eli and the Octopus: The CEO Who Tried to Reform One of the World's Most Notorious Corporations
Imperial Life in the Emerald City: Inside Iraq's Green Zone
Off-Earth: Ethical Questions and Quandaries for Living in Outer Space
I'm Starting to Worry About This Black Box of Doom
Review: I'm Starting To Worry About This Black Box Of Doom by Jason Pargin--Simultaneously hilarious, touching, and a satire of many Internet subcultures, this book is delightful. It follows a young man who is paid to drive a woman and her mysterious box across the country, and the ensuing Internet fallout. As a thriller, it's engaging and unexpected, as a satire, it's scathing and poignant. Thumbs up.

Title: Founding Brothers: The Revolutionary Generation
Review: Founding Brothers by Joseph J. Ellis--I think something that shines through in Ellis's history of a fledgling Republic is how documented their efforts were. That is, they wrote and maintained records at the time with an eye on preserving them for history. Today, it would be equivalent to my shipping my Instagram posts to an archivist as soon as I get elected County Assessor, confident in their future significance. Certainly for the Founders, they were proved correct. I think Ellis also successfully brings out the inherent fragility of the hard-won American experiment as ideologies, groups, and individuals all expressed their beliefs under the banner of "freedom", the meaning of which we still debate today. I enjoyed the book, but recognize it's not for everyone. Thumbs up.

Title: Careless People: A Cautionary Tale of Power, Greed, and Lost Idealism
Number Go Up: Inside Crypto's Wild Rise and Staggering Fall
Review: Number Go Up by Zeke Faux--A critical look at the crypto book, Faux goes from Bitcoin conference to megayacht to Cambodian cyber slaves in pursuit of the crypto phenomenon. Honestly the book has some truly astonishing anecdotes, mostly from traders and exchange heads who are frighteningly blasé about the scope of their fraud. This book is a point in time snapshot of a hype cycle in process, simultaneously documenting his dogged pursuit of what actually was under girding some scams like Tether and FTX. Thumbs up.

Title: Under the Influence: Putting Peer Pressure to Work
The Hidden Girl and Other Stories
Review: The Hidden Girl and Other Stories by Ken Liu--There's a great through line in this collection of short stories. Liu celebrates the small and impactful, whether it's a book set in the far future after the decimation of everything we know or the distant past when a mysterious artifact can impact the world. They vary in quality, certainly, but generally meet a high standard. In particular, I really enjoyed the story "Seven Birthdays" about a girl who grows up with a mother who largely remains in stasis for most of her life. Thumbs up.

Title: In Defense of Elitism: Why I'm Better Than You and You Are Better Than Someone Who Didn't Buy This Book
Coup de Grâce
Review: Coup De Grâce by Sofia Ajram--This book is probably best described as a mediation on suicidal ideation and depression. It cuts deep, down to the individual words used, and is powerful as a piece of experimental horror. Thumbs up.

Title: Seveneves
Review: Seveneves by Neal Stephenson--In some ways a train wreck in slow motion, in others an optimistic look at the nature of humanity, this book defies both expectations and brevity. It's a story told in three parts about the end of the world as we currently know it, and the journey to attempt to build a new society. Stephenson gets in the weeds discussing the nature and source of power in society, requirements for life to succeed and thrive in challenging situations, and perhaps chiefly, the science involved in moving humanity to space using current technology on extremely short notice. Through it all, Stephenson writes with wit, imagination, and delightful threads of connectivity that weave nearly 900 pages of story together. Two thumbs up.

Title: Nuking the Moon: And Other Intelligence Schemes and Military Plots Left on the Drawing Board
Wake
Review: This book is dreamy, literally, moving erratically from the protagonist's waking world to the dreams of those around her. The pacing is fast, but I just wasn't captured by the story, and the stakes were set up at the very end of the first book, which doesn't really have me wanting more. The book is perfectly fine. Sideways thumb.

Title: An Oral History of Atlantis: Stories
Ghost Fleet: A Novel of the Next World War
A People's Guide to Capitalism: An Introduction to Marxist Economics
Review: A People's Guide to Capitalism--Look, I'm certainly susceptible to economic critique from the left (and the right). Done well, a leftist critique humanizes the arcane financial system while setting a clear path forward. Done poorly, it results in this book. Troublingly off-base on even basic facts around capitalism and how/why it persists, and lacking any sort of alternate path because Marxism is verboten, this book languishes, struggling to get to a clear point, and avoiding at every turn accuracy in favor of sensation. Thumbs down.

Title: The Declassification Engine: What History Reveals About America's Top Secrets
The Doctor Who Fooled the World: Andrew Wakefield's war on vaccines
Review: The Doctor Who Fooled The World by Brian Deer--This largely unprecedented examination of what be of the most miscited studies in medical history is jaw-dropping yet matter of fact both in scope and in what it reveals. Deer has been on the medical investigative journalism beat for years, uncovering stories about Big Pharma and biotech hucksters alike. But his examination of Andrew Wakefield and his infamous Lancet study claiming a link between the MMR vaccine regimen and autism is incredibly comprehensive. Deer exposes, for example, Wakefield's compromised approach from the start, which went carefully hidden for years. Deer writes with barely-contained disdain about Wakefield, who he views as intentionally and maliciously pulling the wool over desperate parents' eyes time and time again for decades, with Deer's conclusions, proven out time and time again in court, still explained away by Wakefield's most vocal champions. I knew from mainstream coverage of the Lancet study that the conclusions drawn from such a small sample size shouldn't be trusted, I didn't realize that they were so woefully compromised from the get-go. Very frankly, even the publication of this thorough a takedown of a figure who has proven to be extraordinarily litigious attempting to protect his fraudulent claims seems to demonstrate its own veracity. Thumbs up.

Title: The Secret History of Magic: The True Story of the Deceptive Art
Illuminae
Review:
I wish I had read this as a physical book. 

The beginning of the book, I didn't understand why this had to take place in the future at all, but at the end, it was justified.

Title: Don't Believe It
Review: Don't Believe It by Charlie Donlee--I think this book scratched an itch I didn't know I had. I think there's some ethical problems with exploiting cold cases and the underlying trauma behind them in search of entertainment. But a fictional crime and a fictional true crime documentary? That's something even Kant wouldn't wag a finger at. Focusing on a presumably solved murder in St. Lucia and a documentary maker who desires success, the book is full of fun twists and turns. Thumbs up.

Title: Tracers in the Dark: The Global Hunt for the Crime Lords of Cryptocurrency
How Infrastructure Works: Inside the Systems That Shape Our World
Cue the Sun!: The Invention of Reality TV
Review: Cue The Sun by Emily Nussbaum--Insightful and poignant at times, this book explores the phenomenon of reality TV, suggesting, in part, that maybe the real reality TV was inside us all along. Nussbaum explores what really is a dark history of American reality TV. She develops quick snapshots of major personalities both in front of and behind the camera of prank shows, talent shows, competition shows, fashion shows, and more, taking care to place these shows carefully in the context from which they came. Her interest in reality TV clearly stops at the mid 2000s, though she makes an exception for President Trump and his rise as a Burnett-fueled personality. The way Nussbaum sets out the chronology is about as good as she could get given the circumstances, as well as the relative boom in productions around 2001. The book humanizes a genre of TV that's often dismissed out of hand, all the while, it holds up a dark mirror to the viewing public, and exactly how much exploitation we give ourselves "permission" to enjoy. Thumbs up.

Title: A View from the Stars
Review: A View From The Stars by Cixin Liu--Not wholly breaking new ground here, this book is a collection of short stories and Liu's own thoughts on fiction and the world in general. Here, he elaborates on common themes across his books, expressing broad theories that tend to govern his writing. It's a short simple aggregation. Thumbs up.

Title: Exhalation
Review: Exhalation by Ted Chiang--This collection of short stories runs the spectrum in both length and subject. From the profound to the humorous, Chiang maintains a tight perspective on the human (or inhuman?) condition throughout. It's impressive and worthwhile. Thumbs up.

Title: My Husband
Review: My Husband by Maud Ventura--Anxiety reigns supreme in this relationship/suspense novel. I was struck by the tension around the mundane in this book. Ventura does a good job of putting the reader in the protagonist's shoes, as uncomfortable and ill-fitting as they may be. The climactic reveal of this story is not the most satisfying, but the book itself is interesting. Thumbs up.

Title: Public Citizens: The Attack on Big Government and the Remaking of American Liberalism
The Exceptions: Nancy Hopkins, MIT, and the Fight for Women in Science
A Light in Dark Times: The New School for Social Research and Its University in Exile
The Attention Merchants: The Epic Scramble to Get Inside Our Heads
Gregor and the Marks of Secret
How to Think Like an Economist: Great Economists Who Shaped the World and What They Can Teach Us
Abundance
Review: Abundance by Ezra Klein and Derek Thompson--Powerful and incisive, this book explores a simple question: Why have we (the American government and people) chosen scarcity. Throughout, the authors make a case for choosing abundance as a philosophy while diving into the often baffling local, state, and federal decision-making that prevents it. The realms receiving the most coverage here are health, housing, and power generation, but the implications are ubiquitous, as the authors make clear in the opening paragraphs. I'll often screenshot particularly compelling paragraphs or sentences for books I enjoy, for this book, sharing all the screenshots would exceed attachment limits for most platforms where I share reviews. This is a short book that packs a walloping punch to the status quo. Read it. Two thumbs up.

Title: The Coming Wave: Technology, Power, and the Twenty-first Century's Greatest Dilemma
Review: The Coming Wave by Mustafa Suleyman--AI proponents have a tough balance to strike when advocating for basically any change to the status quo. I'm not certain how well that balance is struck here, by the cofounder of DeepMind. Perhaps characteristically, Suleyman advocates for little nation-state regulation or intervention, since we wouldn't want to stymie progress. At the same time, he is downright alarmist about the negative possibilities of AI. He seems to want to have his cake and eat it too, warn about AI's dangers while hem-hawing about the solution (he does propose some solutions but they're mostly things like "the industry should agree on norms) while criticizing folks who are doing the same thing, like those who call for AI regulation. I'm not quite sure what to make of this. Sideways thumb.
